1.html:
"Arbitrage" is a promise of a free lunch: the same cash flow trading at two prices, bought cheap and sold dear in the same breath, with no risk carried in between. Statistical arbitrage keeps the spirit and quietly deletes the guarantee. Nothing forces convergence here - no shared coupon, no settlement, no law. There is only a statistical relationship between two assets that has reverted often enough, fast enough, in the past that betting on it again has positive expectancy: after costs, most of the time, on average. Every one of those qualifiers is a place the trade can die. This first chapter is about learning to see them - and to tell a relationship that is real from one your eyes invented.

We build from the floor up: a clean synthetic example you can stare at until the mechanics are obvious, then the same picture on a real NSE pair the data itself nominates - where, fair warning, it does not behave. Then the family tree of how the idea scales, what "market neutral" actually buys you, and an unsentimental look at why most of the historical edge is gone. Every number below is computed from the data in front of us, true in this data window and nowhere promised beyond it.

Trade the relationship, not the price

A single stock price is, to a very good first approximation, a random walk: tomorrow is today plus noise, with no centre of gravity pulling it back. Forecasting the level is forecasting that noise - a fool's errand dressed up as analysis. So statistical arbitrage refuses to forecast a level at all. It hunts instead for a combination of two (or many) prices whose difference is stationary: it wanders away from a mean and gets dragged back. When two names share the same underlying drivers, each price inherits the random walk, but the shared trend cancels in the spread, leaving only a mean-reverting wobble. That wobble - not the price - is the thing you trade.

Before touching a real ticker, let us manufacture the ideal case in numpy so the mechanics are unambiguous. We build a shared stochastic trend - a random walk standing in for "whatever moves the whole sector" - and a mean-reverting wobble (an AR(1) process that always leans back toward zero). Price X is the trend plus tiny noise; price Y is the trend plus the wobble. By construction they are cointegrated: each wanders like a random walk, but spread = Y - X is the stationary wobble alone. The Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test - the workhorse stationarity test of the whole series - puts a number on it.

Read the printout, not the picture. The ADF p-value on the price X is 0.465 - high, so we cannot reject the random-walk null; the level is genuinely unforecastable. The ADF p-value on the spread is 0.000 - it emphatically rejects the random walk and declares the spread stationary. The spread's half-life of mean reversion is 8.3 days: that is how long it takes, on average, to close half a deviation, and it is the natural holding period of the trade. The top panel is hopeless to forecast - both lines climb, fall and drift with no anchor. The bottom panel is a different kind of object: it breathes around a fixed mean, and the dotted bands are where a trader would fade it. Everything else in this course is engineering around finding, trusting and trading that bottom panel.

Key idea

Statistical arbitrage trades a stationary relationship, not a price. You give up forecasting the level - which is a random walk - in exchange for forecasting a spread that has a centre of gravity. The entire edge lives or dies on whether that spread is actually stationary out of sample, not just in the sample you measured.

The same picture on real NSE data

Synthetic data was built to cooperate. Real prices were not. Rather than cherry-pick a pretty pair by hand, we let the data nominate one: among names in the same sector - a real economic reason to co-move - with a long overlapping history, which pair's daily returns are the most correlated? High return-correlation is necessary but nowhere near sufficient for a tradable spread, but it is the honest place to start looking.

The data's pick is BAJFINANCE vs BAJAJFINSV - two NBFC/financial names, return-correlation 0.824 over a 2473-day window from mid-2016 to mid-2026. Same group, same sector, deeply intertwined businesses. If anything has a clean spread, surely this does.

It does not. The two names clearly travel together - that is the rebased top panel - but the spread in the bottom panel is nothing like the synthetic one. It has fat excursions, the mean visibly drifts across the window, and the verdict is brutal: the ADF p-value on the spread is 0.199. That is far above 0.05; we cannot reject the random walk. The most return-correlated same-sector pair in this universe does not produce a cleanly stationary spread in this window. And the half-life is 104 days - not the synthetic's tidy eight. A trade in this spread is a position you would carry for months, during which the relationship has ample time to quietly break.

Heads up

High return-correlation and a tradable spread are different altitudes. BAJFINANCE and BAJAJFINSV correlate at 0.824 in daily returns, yet their level spread fails the stationarity test (ADF p = 0.199) in this window. Two names can move together day-to-day while their spread drifts off without ever coming back. Correlation tells you they wiggle in sync; cointegration - covered properly later in the series - tells you whether the spread comes home. Confusing the two is the single most common way a "pairs idea" turns out to be a slow directional bet in disguise.

Tip

The half-life is the trade's natural holding period and a risk gauge in its own right. A half-life of 104 days means a deviation takes months to revert on average - months of margin, financing, borrow and headline risk on two legs. Short half-lives are tradable and forgiving; long ones ask you to be right and patient and solvent the whole time. Always read the half-life before you fall in love with a spread.

The family tree: one engine, a widening cross-section

Pairs trading is the famous case, but it is one rung on a ladder. Move right and you add names and strip out directional risk; the underlying bet - a stationary combination deviates and then reverts - never changes.

Single-name reversion trades one series against its own moving average or fair level - fragile, because a lone price has no economic anchor forcing it back. Pairs add a second name and a single hedge ratio, with an economic story (same sector, shared input costs) supplying the anchor. Baskets trade one name against a weighted basket of peers - a whole cointegrating vector instead of a single ratio - and are more robust, because an idiosyncratic shock to any one peer washes out. Cross-sectional / factor-neutral stat arb ranks the entire universe by a reversion signal, goes long the cheap tail and short the rich tail, and hedges out sector and market factors. That last form is what the large quant books actually run; pairs is the teaching case because you can see every moving part.

Market neutrality: which risk you choose to delete

A long/short book is sold as "market-neutral," but neutrality is a choice of which risk to remove, not a free property of being hedged. There are two common targets, and they are not the same.

Dollar-neutral means equal rupees long and short - net cash exposure zero. But if the long leg has a higher market beta than the short leg, the book still rises and falls with the index; you carry a residual market beta you did not intend. Beta-neutral sizes the short leg by the ratio of betas so the market sensitivities cancel, leaving only the spread's own behaviour - the alpha you actually wanted. Let us put numbers on it for our real pair.

In this window, BAJFINANCE has a market beta of 1.41 and BAJAJFINSV 1.33. Sizing the short at 1.06x the long notional makes the book beta-neutral. The honest detail: because these two betas are close, the naive dollar-neutral book (+1 / -1) carries a residual net beta of only +0.09 here, while the beta-neutral book sits at +0.00 by construction. That small gap is luck of this particular pair - when the legs have betas that diverge, dollar-neutral leaks real index risk, and the residual can be large enough to swamp the spread you were trying to trade. And neither neutrality is permanent: betas drift, so a book that is neutral today leaks exposure tomorrow unless it is re-hedged - and every re-hedge pays costs.

Note

On NSE the short leg is a research abstraction in this course unless you have a genuine implementation path. Shorting equity in the cash market beyond intraday is not free or automatic: it requires stock futures, SLB-borrowed stock, or an intraday square-off, and each choice changes costs, margin, borrow availability, taxes and slippage. We use price series to teach the statistics; turning the short leg into a real position is a separate engineering problem the later chapters take seriously. This is education, not investment advice.

Where the edge came from - and why it decayed

Statistical arbitrage was never magic. Historically it was paid for providing a service the market needed. When a large buyer pushed one name above its peers, the arbitrageur sold the rich name and bought the cheap one, absorbing the imbalance and earning the gap as prices realigned - liquidity provision dressed as a strategy, the bread-and-butter of the pioneering quant desks of the late 1980s and 1990s. News diffused slowly: it hit the obvious name first and its peers lagged, and trading the lag earned the difference. And structural flows - index rebalances, fund redemptions, tax-driven selling - moved spreads for non-fundamental reasons that someone got paid to revert.

Then the edge decayed and crowded. Faster data and execution collapsed the diffusion lag. Thousands of funds now run the same cointegration screens, so deviations are arbitraged away in minutes, not days. Exchange-traded funds and electronic market makers supply much of the liquidity pairs traders once sold. More capital chasing the same finite, shrinking spread means thinner gross returns and brutal sensitivity to costs. The infamous August 2007 "quant quake" - when crowded stat-arb books deleveraged into one another over a few days - is the canonical proof that the edge is shared, correlated and fragile. The chart below makes the point in miniature: the "relationship" you are leaning on is not a constant.

Even our headline pair, supposedly co-moving by nature, has a 1-year rolling return-correlation that ranges from 0.71 to 0.92 across this window, with a standard deviation of 0.05. A pair that looks "cointegrated on average" can be materially less coupled for quarters at a time - and those quarters are exactly when an open position bleeds. The single average correlation of 0.824 we started with hid all of that variation. Averages flatter; the timeline tells the truth.

Honest expectations before you go further

Set the dials correctly now. This is hard, and it is supposed to be - the easy spreads are gone, and what remains is thin, fast and contested by better-capitalised, lower-latency players. Costs dominate: every pair round trip pays statutory charges, spread and impact on two legs, on entry and exit, and a gross edge of a few tens of basis points per trade is routinely negative after costs at retail size. The short leg is rarely free, as noted above. And a statistical relationship existing is not the same as a tradable edge existing - that bright line is the whole reason this module is more than a notebook.

What this series is: research mechanics first - cointegration, spread construction, signal design, honest backtesting, multiple-testing discipline - and execution realism second, layered on so you can watch how much of every paper edge survives contact with reality. The goal is not to sell you a strategy; it is to make you dangerous enough to tell a real one from a backtest artefact.

Where this breaks

  • A stationary past spread guarantees nothing about the future. ADF and cointegration describe the sample you ran them on. Relationships break on mergers, regulation, capital-structure changes and regime shifts - often precisely when your position is open. Our chosen pair already failed the test (p = 0.199) in-sample.
  • The hedge ratio is estimated, not given. The OLS beta of 1.153 is noisy, look-back dependent and drifts; a wrong or stale ratio turns a "market-neutral" spread into a directional bet.
  • Return correlation is the wrong altitude. It is necessary, not sufficient. Two names can correlate at 0.824 in returns and still have a non-stationary, untradeable level spread. We use it here only to start looking; later chapters replace it with a proper cointegration test.
  • Survivorship and point-in-time bias. The universe is today's index members looking back. Pairs that blew up and were delisted never enter the search, flattering every statistic.
  • Crowding makes the edge correlated across funds. When deleveraging hits, "market-neutral" books fall together (August 2007). Neutral to the index is not neutral to other arbitrageurs.
  • Neutrality leaks. Betas drift, so dollar- or beta-neutral today is not neutral next month without re-hedging - and every re-hedge pays costs.

The bottom line: statistical arbitrage is the discipline of trading a stationary relationship you have measured honestly, sized for the risk you chose to keep, in full knowledge that the relationship is estimated, decaying and shared with everyone else running the same screen. The next chapters draw the bright line between a statistical relationship and a tradable edge, and turn the loose word "related" into the precise, testable machinery of cointegration.

2.html:
Every other chapter in this course opens a price panel in a fraction of a second and gets straight to the statistics. That speed is a lie of omission. Somebody had to pull ten years of daily bars for fifty names plus the index, once, and pin them to a local database so that nobody ever has to ask the broker for them again. This chapter is that somebody. It is the unglamorous plumbing under the whole module: where the data comes from, how it is stored, and - the part most tutorials skip - how you prove the cache is actually complete before you trust a single spread built on top of it. Get this wrong and every elegant cointegration test downstream is computing beautiful statistics on quietly broken data.

Download once, read fast

The most common way to waste a research afternoon is to re-download history on every run. A single ten-year daily series is a few hundred kilobytes over the wire - trivial. But fifty of them, pulled repeatedly across a session of iterating on ideas, is thousands of networked round-trips you never needed to make, against an API that is rate-limited and will eventually push back.

The fix is a local cache. You pay the download cost exactly once with source="api"; from then on every source="db" read is a local columnar scan with no network anywhere in the path. OpenAlgo's store is a DuckDB file on disk, and the two regimes look like this - the slow, networked first run, and the instant steady state every run after.

The fifty names and the index

The universe is the fifty current Nifty 50 constituents plus the NIFTY index itself, grouped by sector so that same-sector pairs - the natural hunting ground for a mean-reverting spread - sit next to each other. In this build that is fifty equities across fourteen sectors: five private banks, the NBFC and insurance block, IT, metals, energy, autos, pharma, FMCG, materials, infra and the rest. The equities live on exchange NSE; the index lives on its own exchange, NSE_INDEX, which matters because you will fetch it with a different argument and it is your calendar ground truth later.

One word of warning you should hear now and again at the end: this is today's membership, cached backwards. That single fact is the most flattering bias in the entire course, and we return to it with teeth in the final section.

db vs api, measured

An expert does not take "the cache is faster" on faith. Time the identical call both ways on one liquid name, a few repetitions each, and report the measured ratio. The api path crosses the network to the broker and re-serialises the response; the db path is a local DuckDB query. Absolute numbers depend on your machine and the broker's mood, but the shape never changes.

In this data window the db read came back in a median 54.7 ms against the api path's 346.0 ms - the cache is 6.3x faster on a single ten-year daily series. Multiply that by fifty names across a session of dozens of re-runs and the difference stops being a convenience and becomes the difference between an idea you can iterate on and one you abandon out of boredom.

Key idea

The speed-up is not the point - reproducibility is. A source="db" read returns the same bytes every time, so a backtest you ran last month reruns identically today. A source="api" read can drift as the vendor revises history underneath you. Cache first, then research.

Inside the cache: the market_data table

OpenAlgo stores every bar in a DuckDB table called market_data, one row per (symbol, exchange, interval, timestamp), where timestamp is a Unix epoch in seconds - integer keys are small, sort fast, and never trip over a timezone. A companion data_catalog table summarises each series (first timestamp, last timestamp, record count, last download time) so coverage can be checked without scanning the bars themselves. Writing a downloaded OHLCV frame into that schema is a tidy transform - cast the index to epoch seconds, coerce volume and open interest to integers - followed by an insert. Reading the catalog back out is a single GROUP BY symbol that any verification routine can trust.

Note

The catalog is not decoration. Treat it as the cache's manifest: the cheapest possible "what do I actually have?" query. Every coverage check below could be run straight off data_catalog without touching a single price bar - which is exactly how a nightly job confirms a download finished before research opens the file.

Verifying coverage: rows, spans, and gaps

A download that ran is not a download that succeeded. Before trusting fifty series for research you verify every one - row count, first date, last date - and flag anything short or missing. Crucially this verification uses source="db" only: a pure local scan, no further downloads. The first view is simply how many daily bars each name carries.

A healthy daily cache is a flat block of bars sitting against the same ceiling, with a handful of short stubs. In this window the index calendar prints 2,477 sessions and the full-history names sit right up against it; the stubs - JIOFIN, ETERNAL, TMPV, SHRIRAMFIN - are genuinely younger series, recent listings or symbol renames, not data errors. You confirm that by looking at when each series starts, not just how many rows it has.

Row counts tell you how many bars; the span view tells you which window. Each bar runs from a series' first cached date to its last. Across this cache the earliest start is 2016-01-01 and every series ends on or around 2026-06-25 - the last common trading day. That alignment on the right edge is the thing you are checking for. A bar that stopped early would be a stale series nobody topped up, and on a two-legged spread a stale leg is not a small problem - it silently freezes one side of the relationship while the other keeps moving.

But row counts and spans can both look healthy and still hide internal holes - a missing Tuesday here, a skipped week there - that quietly corrupt a spread without ever showing up as a short history. To find them you need the set of dates the market was actually open. Rather than hardcode a holiday list that rots, use the NIFTY index series as the ground-truth NSE session calendar: the index prints on every trading day, by definition. For each symbol, within its own active window, a gap is any NIFTY session it is missing.

The verdict in this data window: against a 2,477-session calendar, 10 of 50 names are perfectly clean, and the worst single name is missing just 3 sessions. Three. Those are almost certainly real trading halts or suspensions, not corruption - the kind of thing a sane cache lives with. But notice what this check buys you: it is the difference between "the file loaded" and "the file is right." If one name had been missing forty sessions, the row count alone might never have flagged it, and you would have discovered the hole the hard way, as a fake jump in a spread weeks later.

Heads up

A single bad or missing bar on one leg is far more dangerous in stat-arb than in directional trading. A spread is a difference; a hole on one side fabricates a move that never happened. Always re-run gap detection after any bulk download - the verification pass, not the download log, is your proof the cache is sound.

Keeping it current without re-downloading

Once the cache exists, keeping it current must be cheap. The wrong way is to re-download ten years every morning. The right way is to ask the cache for each symbol's last date, fetch only [last+1 .. today] from the API, and append with de-duplication so an overlapping re-fetch costs nothing.

Tip

Build the loop to over-fetch on purpose - re-pull the last few weeks every night, not just yesterday. The de-dup throws away what you already have, and the overlap quietly heals any bar the vendor revised after you first cached it. The cost is a few extra kilobytes; the benefit is a cache that self-corrects.

And because a real download loop hits the API hundreds of times, wrap every fetch in exponential backoff - on a timeout or a too many requests, wait, then double the wait a few times before giving up. It is ten lines of code and the difference between a download that survives a flaky network and one that dies half-written at symbol thirty.

Where this breaks

This cache is a convenience, not a source of truth. The honest failure modes:

  • Rate limits and partial downloads. A fifty-symbol pull is hundreds of API calls, and a mid-run 429 or timeout can leave a series half-written. The retry wrapper helps; the real guard is the verification pass above. Never assume a bulk download finished - re-verify coverage and gaps every time.
  • Adjustment is baked in at download time. The bars are whatever the broker served on the day they were fetched - typically split- and dividend-adjusted to that date. A later corporate action retro-adjusts history at the vendor, so an old cached series can silently disagree with a fresh pull. The cached close is for measuring relationships, not for reconstructing the exact rupee you could have transacted at.
  • Vendor gaps and bad prints. Missing sessions, zero-volume days, the odd spike or stale carry-forward come straight from the upstream feed. Gap detection finds the holes; it does not fix a wrong price. A spread is unforgiving of a single bad bar on one leg.
  • Point-in-time and survivorship. This is today's Nifty 50, cached backwards. Names that were dropped, merged or delisted are simply absent, and recent renames - ETERNAL, TMPV, JIOFIN, SHRIRAMFIN - have discontinuous histories. A backtest on this universe is biased toward survivors. A true point-in-time database with delisted constituents is out of scope here, but it is the limitation that most flatters every result downstream, and you should never forget it is there.

With the database built, verified and kept current, the rest of the course simply reads it with source="db". The next chapter assembles the clean research panel from this cache and states the trading-versus-adjusted-price caveats in full.

3.html:
A statistical-arbitrage book has two ways to die, and only one of them is famous. The famous death is a bad model: a spread that looked stationary in-sample and wandered off the moment real money touched it. The quieter death, the one that kills far more research before it ever reaches a model, is the data layer underneath. A misaligned date, a symbol that quietly changed names, a backtest filled at a price nobody could have traded, a universe that silently excludes everything that went to zero - none of these throw an error. They just hand you a Sharpe ratio that is too good and let you discover the truth in production. This chapter builds the data layer the whole module leans on, and names every bias in it out loud, because an honest expert spends more time distrusting the panel than admiring the strategy.

The aligned price panel

Everything downstream reads off one object: an aligned panel of daily closes, rows indexed by trading day, columns by symbol. In this data window that panel is 3,195 trading days by 50 names, spanning 2016-01-01 to 2026-06-25, with the NIFTY index carried alongside as a market factor. All 50 of 50 names load; anything that fails is printed, never silently dropped. That single matrix is the substrate for every cointegration test, every spread, every z-score in the chapters that follow - which is exactly why an error here is the most dangerous error you can make. It propagates into every statistic at once, and it does so without a single warning.

The trap hides in the word aligned. Real names do not all start trading on the same day, and the exchange calendar has holidays and half-days that not every symbol observes identically. Build the panel on the union of all dates and the ragged edges show up as NaNs; reach for a blind dropna() to tidy them and you have silently shrunk your sample to the intersection - often without noticing how much you threw away or which name forced the cut.

Key idea

The aligned panel is the single substrate of the whole module. Get it wrong and every cointegration test, spread and z-score is wrong together, silently. Treat the panel build with more suspicion than the strategy - that is where expert time actually pays off.

The real bar: years of history per name

Before any pair test, ask the unglamorous question: how much history does each name actually have? Rebasing and ranking the first valid date per column gives the honest answer.

Most of the universe carries the full ~10.5 years, but the short tail is the part that matters: JIOFIN at 2.8 years, ETERNAL at 4.9, MAXHEALTH at 5.8, HDFCLIFE at 8.6, SBILIFE at 8.7. These are not rounding noise; they dictate what you can test. A cointegration relationship wants a long, regime-spanning overlap. If you demand a rectangular panel with no NaNs, the newest listing caps the common window for every pair it touches - pair anything with JIOFIN and your usable sample collapses to about 700 trading days. A relationship that holds over 700 days, through essentially one market regime, can very easily be a coincidence dressed as structure.

The other direction is no safer. Drop the short-history names to keep a long window and you have quietly selected the oldest, most-established large caps - a survivorship-flavoured bias before you have run a single statistic. There is no free choice here. You pick which bias you accept and you write it down.

Heads up

2.8 years is roughly 700 observations covering one regime. Engle-Granger and Johansen will happily return a "significant" result on a window that short. Significance on a single regime is not evidence of a tradable, persistent relationship - it is the most common way a stat-arb backtest lies to its author.

The liquidity filter: richest vs thinnest

Statistical arbitrage lives or dies on the ability to trade both legs cheaply. Rank the universe by average daily traded value - close times volume, averaged over the trailing 365 days, expressed in rupees crore - and you get the order in which names will cost you to transact.

Here is the brutally honest part of this window. The thinnest five names are GRASIM at Rs 203 crore/day, SBILIFE and JSWSTEEL at Rs 198 crore, NESTLEIND at Rs 185 crore, and TATACONSUM at Rs 164 crore. The thinnest name in the entire universe still turns over more than a hundred and sixty crore rupees a day. There is no genuinely illiquid name on this list - it is fifty large caps. So in this window the liquidity filter is discipline, not rescue: it tells you which pairs will cost more in spread, not which pairs are untradeable. The real liquidity trap - a backtest that fills at a printed close no one could have transacted - bites hardest in a wider mid- and small-cap universe, exactly the universe a hungry researcher is tempted to reach for to find more pairs.

Tip

A pair is only as liquid as its thinner leg. The cheaper-to-trade side does not subsidise the expensive one; the thin side sets the spread and impact you pay on the whole position. Rank by traded value and feed it in as a cost input to pair selection, not as a footnote after the fact.

Co-movement is necessary, not sufficient

Rebasing each name to 100 at the start of the window shows, at a glance, how members of a sector travel together. This is the visual intuition behind the entire module - and also its single most seductive trap.

The bank lines move together; the IT lines move together; metals and energy each cluster. The eye reads "these belong in a pair" and the wallet gets ready to pay. But shared movement is correlation, and correlation is necessary and nowhere near sufficient for a tradable spread. An entire sector can drift upward together - both legs riding the same beta - while a long/short pair built on them mean-reverts around nothing you can monetise, or worse, the spread itself trends and never comes back. Two lines climbing in parallel share a direction; that is not the same as a spread whose level stays bounded. The gap between those two ideas is the whole reason the cointegration machinery exists, and it is where most pairs that "look obvious" on a rebased chart die.

Note

Correlation describes returns moving together day to day. Cointegration describes the level of a spread staying bounded over time. You can have high correlation with a spread that drifts away forever, and modest correlation with a beautifully stationary spread. The chart shows the first; only a stationarity test shows the second.

Adjusted prices measure, tradable prices fill

The closes in this panel are split- and dividend-adjusted, which is correct for measuring a relationship: adjustment strips out the artificial cliffs a split or large dividend would otherwise punch into the series, so the spread reflects economics rather than corporate-action bookkeeping. But you can never transact at an adjusted price. The fill happens on the raw tradable close, which carries the jump. A corporate action can shift a hedge ratio overnight, so the ratio you estimated last week may be stale the morning after an ex-date.

Every backtest in this module trades adjusted closes for cleanliness. That is a deliberate, disclosed simplification: real fills land on raw prices, with a spread and market impact on top, and the reconciliation between the two is work that has to happen before a relationship becomes a position.

Survivorship and the symbol-change trap

This panel is today's large-cap members, looking backward - and that single sentence contains two biases an expert never skips. First, survivorship. Names that were dropped from the index, merged away, or delisted are simply absent. Absence is not neutral; it is optimistic, because the names that fell out are disproportionately the ones that did badly. A backtest run on survivors flatters every statistic it touches. Second, the symbol-change trap. A company that demerged, merged, or rebranded reappears under a new ticker with a truncated history - several of the short-history names above are partly this artifact. A naive loader either chops the past off or, worse, stitches two different economic entities into one continuous series and calls it a price.

A true point-in-time universe would reconstruct index membership through time and carry the delisted names alongside the survivors. That is out of scope for this module, and so the honest move is not to pretend the bias is gone but to state it plainly and keep it in view whenever a result looks suspiciously clean.

Where this breaks

  • Survivorship and point-in-time bias. The panel is current members looking backward; dropped, merged and delisted names are absent, and their absence flatters every statistic. We name it; we do not correct it here.
  • Symbol changes. Demergers, mergers and rebrands leave discontinuous histories under new tickers. The short-history tail (JIOFIN, ETERNAL and friends) is partly this, and a careless loader can stitch two different companies into one series.
  • Short histories cap the trustworthy window. A rectangular panel is only as long as its newest listing; 2.8 years through one regime is not enough to trust a cointegration result, however significant it prints.
  • Stale or thin prints. This universe is uniformly liquid, so the liquidity filter is discipline rather than rescue - but in any wider universe, low-volume names carry prices you could not actually trade, and the filter is a partial defence, not a cure.
  • Adjusted prices hide the real fill. The module models adjusted closes and fills, in reality, on raw prices with a spread and impact. The gap is widest exactly at corporate actions, where hedge ratios also move.

With the data layer built and its limits stated, the next chapter draws the line that the rebased sector charts only hint at: the difference between a statistical relationship you can see and a tradable edge you can actually hold - before we touch a single strategy.

4.html:
Every chart, test and Sharpe ratio in this course is computed on a long/short equity spread that buys one stock and shorts another on the close. That trade is a research abstraction. It exists cleanly in a notebook and almost nowhere else. The long leg is trivial - you buy the stock for delivery and hold it. The short leg is where the abstraction walks out of the notebook and into an Indian trading account, and meets compulsory intraday square-off, borrow scarcity, futures basis, position-limit bans, margin calls, price bands and physical settlement. None of those frictions live inside a close-price series, which means none of them live inside any backtest built on one. This chapter is the lens you carry into every later chapter, and it compresses to a single sentence: a statistical relationship existing is not the same as a tradable edge existing - and almost the entire distance between the two is the short leg.

The backtest makes three quiet assumptions

When a later chapter buys stock A, shorts stock B on the close, and tracks the spread back to its mean, it is leaning on three assumptions it never states out loud. First, that you can stay short B for as long as the reversion takes - days, sometimes weeks. Second, that both legs fill at the price you measured. Third, that carrying the position costs nothing. Every one of those is false in an Indian account, and each falsehood has a name. You cannot hold a naked cash short overnight. You measured an adjusted close and you fill on the next bar at a raw price with a spread and impact. And the short leg drags a borrow fee, a futures basis, or a roll cost that appears nowhere in the price series. The diagram is the mental model for the whole chapter: a clean research lane on top, and the messy real-market lane the short leg falls into underneath.

Why we learn on the close and can never trade it

The statistics in this course are built on daily closing prices, and that choice is correct for learning and wrong for executing at the same time. The closing print is the cleanest, most comparable, least-noisy point of the day: one auction-settled number per name, split- and dividend-adjusted so a relationship is measured rather than shattered by a corporate action, and aligned across all fifty names onto a single date index - exactly the matrix a cointegration test wants. It is the right learning signal. It is also a price you can never transact at. There is no bid-ask inside an adjusted close, no depth, no impact, and the closing auction may not absorb your size. Your signal fires at the close and your real fill lands on the next bar at a raw price. Holding both ideas at once - the close is the right thing to model and the wrong thing to trade - is the discipline the whole module is built on.

Note

An adjusted close is a measurement instrument, not a tradable quote. Use it to decide whether a relationship exists; never assume you could have transacted at it. The series you model and the series you fill on diverge most violently at exactly the corporate actions that adjustment papers over.

The short leg is the whole problem

Carrying a short in India is not one decision; it is a choice among four instruments, each swapping the clean equity exposure of the backtest for a different vehicle and a different bag of frictions.

Read the rows honestly. An intraday cash short lets you sell a stock you do not own, but only intraday - the broker force-squares it near the close, which makes overnight mean-reversion pairs impossible to carry this way and the spread-plus-impact bill payable twice every day. SLB, securities lending and borrowing, is the cleanest economic match to a true short: you borrow the stock, sell it, return it later, and pay an annualized borrow fee. But Indian SLB is thin and lumpy - many names have little or no borrow, the fee spikes precisely when everyone wants the same short, and the lender can recall mid-trade. Stock futures are the realistic overnight default: a single deeply liquid instrument in large names, naturally short-able, where the price you pay is the basis, a roll cost every expiry, lot-size granularity that stops you sizing to the rupee, margin, and ban risk. A synthetic short through options replicates the exposure but adds two option legs, Greeks and assignment risk, and for a linear spread it is rarely worth the complexity. The uncomfortable part: the hedge ratio you so carefully estimated on equity closes is now implemented in a different instrument, and that mismatch is tracking error you simply did not have in the backtest.

Tip

The backtest shorts an equity; you will most often short a future. That substitution is invisible in the curve but real in the account - a future has its own basis, its own roll, and a lot size you cannot trim. Decide the short vehicle for each pair before you trust the backtest, because the vehicle, not the signal, sets the cost.

Which names can you actually short-size?

F and O eligibility is necessary but not sufficient. A name can be in the derivatives segment and still be too thin to take a real position without moving its own price, so the first filter is liquidity, not statistics. Ranking the universe by average daily traded value - close times volume, in rupees crore - gives a blunt proxy for how much size each leg can absorb.

In this data window the richest name, HDFCBANK, turns over about Rs 2,479 crore a day against the thinnest, TATACONSUM, at about Rs 164 crore - roughly 15x apart inside a single index, with a median name trading near Rs 399 crore/day. Notice what this does and does not buy you. Because the universe is fifty large caps, zero names trade under a third of the median - there is no genuinely untradeable name here, so the filter is discipline rather than rescue: it tells you which pairs will cost more in spread, not which are impossible. The red, thin end is still where the short leg quietly breaks - wider spreads, shallower depth, a less active futures contract, scarcer SLB borrow - and a pair that marries a liquid name to a thin one inherits the thin one's execution problems on both legs.

Eligibility, bans, margin and settlement

Even with a liquid name and a futures contract, four exchange mechanics that appear in no price series decide whether you can hold the trade. Stock futures only exist for names in the F and O segment, and that list is actively curated on liquidity criteria - names enter and exit it, and a stock leaving the list kills its futures short outright. Layered on top is the Market-Wide Position Limit: when aggregate open interest across all participants in a stock crosses 95% of its MWPL, the stock enters a ban period in which only position-reducing trades are allowed. You cannot open or add, and trying draws a penalty. For a market-neutral book this is the worst possible failure mode - your pair can be forced into a one-sided exit precisely when the name is most crowded.

Heads up

An MWPL ban does not arrive when convenient. It triggers when a name is crowded - often the same moment your spread is most stretched and most attractive to add to. If you cannot open or add the short leg, you are holding half a hedge, which is naked directional risk wearing a market-neutral label. Check eligibility and ban status before every entry, not once at backtest time.

The same futures short drags in margin, bands and settlement. A short future ties up SPAN plus exposure margin - often a fifth to a third of notional, per leg - so a pair is two margined legs marked to market daily, and a gap day calls for more cash. In practice it is margin, not your signal, that caps position size. Price bands and circuits can lock a name so you cannot exit one leg, and half a hedge is again naked risk. And settlement differs by instrument: cash equity settles T+1, but stock futures are physically settled if carried into expiry, so holding a short future to the last day means owing delivery of shares. The roll calendar therefore becomes part of the strategy - you must roll or close before every expiry, paying the roll each month.

The basis you never modelled

When you short a stock through its future you do not trade the cash price at all - you trade the futures price, which sits away from spot by the basis. Under simple cost-of-carry the fair future is F = S x exp((r - q) x T), with r the financing rate, q the dividend yield and T the time to expiry. There is no futures series in this database, so the chart below is a model of the fair basis off a real spot, not a market quote - just enough to make the magnitude concrete.

On the real RELIANCE spot in this window, with an illustrative r = 6.5% and q = 0.6%, the modelled 30-day fair basis is about Rs 6.41, or roughly 48.6 bps of spot. Small - but not zero, and that is the point. The basis changes every day, it converges to zero by expiry (a mild tailwind for a short when carry is positive), and it appears nowhere in the equity close series your statistics were built on. So the spread you actually trade on futures is not the spread you cointegrated on cash, and that gap is tracking error. Worse, real quotes embed demand, borrow scarcity and roll pressure on top of textbook carry, so the basis can move against you exactly when a name is hard to borrow - noise your cash-based hedge ratio never saw.

A relationship is not an edge

This is the doctrine of the course in one picture. Thousands of pairs co-move. Far fewer are cointegrated in-sample. Fewer still stay cointegrated out-of-sample. Fewer again survive realistic costs on two legs traded twice. And only what is left at the bottom - cheap to trade, short-able, stable, net-positive after every layer above - is an actual edge. Each chapter from here is one more layer of this funnel.

Key idea

The single sentence to carry through the rest of the course: a statistical relationship existing is not the same as a tradable edge existing. The relationship lives at the top of the funnel and costs nothing to find. The edge lives at the bottom, and the entire descent is paid for in short-leg frictions - borrow, basis, ban risk, margin and cost.

The pre-trade checklist

Before a single rupee of real capital touches a stat-arb idea, it has to pass nine gates. A no on any one of them does not mean trade smaller - it means the idea goes back to research, not to the order book.

The gates are not equally famous, but they are equally fatal. Gates 1 to 3 decide whether the trade is even possible - size, short access, and segment-and-ban status. Gates 4 and 5 decide whether it is profitable once the market takes its cut on two legs traded twice, with next-bar fills and the genuine risk that one leg fills and the other does not. Gates 6 to 9 decide whether you can survive it - margined capital with a gap-day buffer, monitoring that surfaces a broken leg the moment it happens, a kill switch decided before you need it, and an after-tax view, since futures and cash are taxed differently and the difference comes straight out of net edge.

Where this breaks

  • The short leg is assumed, not guaranteed. Every backtest in this course shorts freely. In a real account the short may be un-borrowable, un-shortable overnight, or in an F and O ban exactly when you need it. No short, no market-neutral trade - and that single gate invalidates more "edges" than any statistical test.
  • Instrument mismatch is silent tracking error. Hedge ratios are estimated on cash closes but implemented in futures, with their own basis, roll and lot-size granularity. The thing you trade is never quite the thing you modelled, and the difference does not show up until real money is on it.
  • Costs and carry are off-page. Closing prices contain no spread, no impact, no borrow fee, no basis, no roll, no margin cost. They all subtract from the curve; this chapter only names them, and a later chapter quantifies the damage.
  • Eligibility and limits move underfoot. F and O membership, MWPL bans, price bands and margin rates all change over time. A relationship that was tradable last year can be administratively un-tradable today, with no change in the statistics at all.
  • This chapter proves nothing on its own. It is judgement and diagrams, not a result. The liquidity numbers are illustrative in this data window and the basis figure is a model, not a market quote. Carry it as the lens for every later chapter, not as evidence of an edge - and the next chapter starts drawing the family tree of the strategy now that you know which parts of it are research and which parts are real.

5.html:
A stock falls for three weeks, and somewhere a trader says the words that have funded more blown accounts than any other: "it's oversold, it has to revert." Stop there. Before you can claim a series reverts, you have to prove it has something to revert to — a fixed mean and a stable variance that the next observation is tethered to. Most price series have neither. The whole edifice of statistical arbitrage rests on one property that decides whether "it will revert" is a tradeable statement or a sentence you are projecting onto a chart: stationarity. This chapter shows, on real NSE data, that a single stock price almost never has it — and that its returns almost always do. That asymmetry is not a curiosity. It is the reason stat-arb never trades a price, only a spread it has engineered to be stationary.

The one idea: I(1) prices, I(0) returns

A series is integrated of order d — written I(d) — if you have to difference it d times before it becomes stationary. Two orders matter for everything that follows.

An I(1) series — a price — needs differencing once. The level itself carries a unit root: it wanders with no mean to return to, and its variance grows without bound as time passes. Ask "where should this be?" and there is no honest answer. An I(0) series — a return — is already stationary: a fixed long-run mean (near zero for daily log returns), a finite and roughly stable variance, and an autocorrelation that dies almost immediately. Deviations from its mean are temporary by construction. The single operation that turns the first into the second is the log difference, r_t = log(P_t) − log(P_{t-1}). That is the entire reason returns, not prices, are the well-behaved object — and the reason a tradeable mean-reverting object in this series is never a price, but a constructed spread forced to be I(0).

A random walk and a mean-reverter, built from scratch

Before touching market data, build both objects yourself so there is no ambiguity about what "stationary" looks like. A random walk is x_t = x_{t-1} + e_t: each shock is added into the level forever. This is the textbook I(1) process and the canonical model of an efficient price. A stationary AR(1) is y_t = phi * y_{t-1} + e_t with |phi| < 1: a shock decays geometrically at rate phi, so the series is pulled back toward zero. That is I(0) and the canonical model of a mean-reverting spread.

With a seeded generator the numbers are exact. Over 1,500 steps the random walk drifts to a level mean of −31.88 with a standard deviation of 24.16 — and both numbers are meaningless, because they depend entirely on where the window happens to end. The AR(1) with phi = 0.85 sits at a mean of −0.40 with std 1.74: a real, stable centre of gravity at zero. The single parameter phi is the whole story, and the cruel detail is the boundary: at phi = 1 the AR(1) becomes the random walk. Mean-reversion and non-stationarity are separated by a razor's edge, which is exactly why testing for the difference is hard — a theme that comes back to bite us at the end.

The clearest fingerprint is what happens to the variance over time. Simulate many independent paths and watch the cross-sectional spread at each step. For the random walk, theory says Var(x_t) = t * sigma^2 — it grows linearly forever; in a 400-path run the cross-sectional variance reaches 391.9 by step 399 and is still climbing, the cloud fanning out with no anchor. For the AR(1) the variance converges to sigma^2 / (1 − phi^2) = 3.60, and the simulation settles at 3.56 — the paths stay inside a band. That band is the thing mean reversion trades. A random walk has no band, so there is nothing to trade.

Key idea

"Mean-reverting" is a property of the process, not a hope you attach to a chart. The test is whether the variance is bounded. If the spread of possible future values keeps widening with the horizon, there is no fair value, no anchor, and no edge — only a story.

The same split in a real NSE stock

Now the claim that matters: a real stock price behaves like the random walk, and its log returns behave like the stationary process. Take one liquid name over this data window — RELIANCE, 2,477 daily closes from June 2016 to June 2026 — and plot the level against its log returns.

The price chart has no horizontal line you could honestly draw as "fair value" — wherever it is, it could be twice or half that in a few years. Split the sample in half and the log-price mean shifts from 6.272 to 7.156: the centre of the level moves, which is precisely what non-stationarity looks like. The returns chart is the opposite. Its mean is pinned near zero in both halves (+0.00124 then +0.00023) and its standard deviation is stable (0.0195 then 0.0141). One caveat we will not bury: returns are stationary in mean, but their variance clusters — calm stretches and violent ones. That is conditional heteroskedasticity, not non-stationarity; the long-run variance is still finite and stable. It matters enormously for risk sizing, and we flag it now so it is not a surprise later.

The ADF test, read the way it is meant to be read

Eyeballing charts is not evidence. The Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test is the workhorse. It fits delta y_t = alpha + beta*t + gamma * y_{t-1} + (lagged differences) + e_t and tests H0: gamma = 0 (a unit root — non-stationary) against H1: gamma < 0 (mean-reverting). Two specification choices a professional never leaves on autopilot. First, lag length: the lagged-difference terms soak up short-run autocorrelation so the statistic is valid; too few biases the test, too many wastes power. Use autolag='AIC' to let an information criterion choose. Second, regression specification: 'c' includes a constant (reverts to a non-zero level), 'ct' adds a linear trend, 'n' has neither. And the part most people skip entirely — the test is one-sided and the statistic is negative. You do not compare a p-value to 0.05 and stop; you compare the statistic to the critical values. More negative is stronger evidence against the unit root.

Read it out loud. For RELIANCE's log price, the statistic is −2.234, sitting above (less negative than) even the 10% critical value of −2.567 — we fail to reject the unit root. For its log returns the statistic is −15.241, far below the 1% critical value of −3.433 — we reject decisively. That is the I(1)-price / I(0)-return result, on real data, with the test read correctly. The p-values (0.194 versus 0.000) tell the same story, but the statistic-versus-critical-value framing is the one that survives contact with a borderline series.

Now the discipline most people skip: does the verdict survive different specifications? Re-running the price test across the grid, 'c' with AIC gives −2.234, 'ct' gives −1.997 (and its critical value tightens to −3.412), and the no-constant 'n' spec gives a positive +1.825. Every one says "do not reject" — the non-stationary verdict for this price is robust here. But notice how much the statistic moved, and that the 'n' spec flipped its sign entirely. That robustness is the exception. For the borderline, slow-reverting spreads stat-arb actually finds, the choice of spec routinely flips the answer, and it is tempting to pick the one that tells you what you want.

Heads up

Never choose the regression specification or lag length after seeing the result. With a borderline series you can almost always find a 'c'/'ct'/'n' combination that crosses the 5% line, and the temptation to keep the one that "works" is how stat-arb research quietly fits noise. Fix the spec on theory before you run the test, and report what you fixed.

The ADF has a notorious weakness — it under-rejects — so it is worth running a test whose null is the mirror image. The KPSS test takes H0: stationary; rejecting it is evidence for a unit root. For RELIANCE's log price the KPSS statistic is 7.216, blowing past the 1% critical value of 0.739 — it rejects stationarity. For the returns it is 0.317, below the 5% value of 0.463 — it fails to reject. Both tests now agree from opposite ends, which is the strongest possible verdict.

Tip

Always run ADF and KPSS together. Because ADF under-rejects, "ADF failed to reject" alone is weak. If KPSS also points the same way, you have a real verdict. When the diagonal cells light up — one rejects, the other does not in a contradictory pattern — treat the series as unresolved and re-specify with a trend term before you trust any spread built on it.

One number on the line: the Hurst exponent

ADF and KPSS hand you a yes/no. The Hurst exponent H gives a position on a line and a feel for the strength of the behaviour. Estimate it from how the typical size of moves scales with the horizon: measure the root-mean-square change over lag k and fit rms(k) ~ k^H on a log-log scale. Then H ~ 0.5 means the series diffuses like a random walk; H < 0.5 means anti-persistent and mean-reverting (the shape of a tradeable spread); H > 0.5 means persistent and trending. Validate the estimator on series whose answer we already know: the synthetic random walk returns 0.502, the AR(1) returns 0.049, white noise −0.004, and a pure drift-plus-trend 0.992. The estimator works.

Run it across real NSE log prices and the result is unambiguous: in this window the Hurst exponents land in a tight band of [0.46, 0.56] with a mean of exactly 0.50. No single stock price sits convincingly below 0.5. Not one name is a mean-reverter. This is the same fact the autocorrelation function tells: RELIANCE's log price has a lag-1 autocorrelation of 1.000 and an ACF that decays agonisingly slowly (today looks exactly like yesterday, because shocks never leave), while its log returns have a lag-1 autocorrelation of −0.009 — essentially zero memory from the very first lag. There is nothing in the daily return series to exploit directly, and nothing in the price to revert against.

Note

Read the Hurst exponent as a continuous diagnostic, not a verdict. ADF and KPSS answer "unit root: yes or no"; Hurst tells you where on the line a series sits and roughly how strong the behaviour is. A spread worth trading should land clearly below 0.5 — and the further below, the faster it covers less ground than a random walk would, which is the same as saying it reverts harder.

The whole cross-section agrees

One stock could be a fluke. Sweep 24 liquid NSE names, run ADF (regression='c', autolag='AIC') on each name's log price and its log returns, and collect every verdict in one grid.

The split is near-total. Every single price fails to reject the unit root — 100% of names, with p-values spread from 0.13 (HINDUNILVR, the most borderline) up to 0.95 (BHARTIARTL, M&M, SUNPHARMA). Every single return rejects it — 100% of names, with p-values that all round to 0.000. KPSS, with its opposite null, mirrors the verdict exactly: 100% of names reject stationarity on the price, and only 4% — a single name — reject it on returns. There is no ambiguity in this window. Prices are I(1); returns are I(0); and that is why you cannot mean-revert a single stock. The mean-reverting object has to be manufactured — a spread between two co-moving series, engineered so the spread itself is I(0) even though neither leg is. That construction is the subject of the chapters that follow.

Where this breaks

Stationarity testing feels like a measurement and is anything but. The honest caveats each come back to bite real research.

ADF has low power near a unit root, and this is not a footnote — it is the single most important caveat in applied cointegration work. A genuinely stationary AR(1) with phi close to 1 (slow mean reversion) is one the ADF test routinely cannot distinguish from a random walk in a few thousand observations. Make it concrete: simulate series that are stationary by construction and ask how often ADF correctly rejects.

At phi = 0.80 the test correctly rejects ~100% of the time. At phi = 0.99 — still perfectly stationary — it rejects only ~61%. Roughly two in five times it declares a unit root that is not there. The brutal consequence: "ADF didn't reject" is not proof of a unit root. It is very often just weak data. And the spreads stat-arb actually finds sit exactly in this dangerous region — slow mean-reverters with phi near 1, where the tests are least reliable and where half-lives are long enough that costs eat the edge before reversion arrives.

The other failure modes compound it. Specification sensitivity: 'c' versus 'ct' versus 'n', and the lag length, can flip a borderline verdict — so decide the spec before you look, never after. Structural breaks: every test here assumes one fixed regime. A pair that was stationary for three years and broke after a merger or an index reconstitution will still test "stationary" on the pooled sample while being untradeable now; stationarity over the whole window says nothing about stationarity going forward. Finite samples and fat tails: critical values are asymptotic, daily equity returns have heavy tails and volatility clustering, and the KPSS p-value is clipped to a small lookup table — so treat every p-value as a fuzzy guide, not a number. And the deepest caveat of all: stationary is necessary, not sufficient. Even a perfect I(0) spread is not a strategy. It still has to revert faster than costs accrue, survive out of sample, and be implementable on both legs in the Indian market — the gap this whole series exists to expose.

6.html:
Every blown-up pairs book starts the same way. Someone runs a correlation screen, finds two names that move together like twins, shorts the rich one against the cheap one, and waits for the gap to close. It does not. The gap widens. They average down. It widens more. Eventually the desk pulls the position and the post-mortem reads the same every time: the pair decorrelated. It never did. The correlation was real the whole way down. It was simply never the thing that makes a spread come back. This is the hinge of the entire course, and if you keep one idea from it, keep this: correlation is not cointegration, and confusing the two is how statistical-arbitrage traders die.

The mistake almost everyone makes

Correlation answers a short-term question: did these two names move in the same direction on the same days? It is measured on returns, it is bounded between minus one and plus one, and it is genuinely useful for one thing only - building a hedge that does not lurch around day to day. What it cannot tell you is whether the gap between two prices will ever come back. Two stocks can post a +0.8 return correlation for a decade while their price ratio drifts steadily from 1.0 to 3.0 and never returns. Every daily wiggle agrees; the long-run levels walk away from each other forever.

Cointegration answers the question you actually care about: is there a specific combination of these two prices that is tethered to a mean? It is measured on price levels (log levels, to be exact), and a "yes" means the spread between the legs is stationary - it has a centre of gravity it keeps falling back toward. That mean is the only thing a pairs trade leans on. No stationary spread, no reversion, no trade - however gorgeous the correlation looks on the screen.

These are not two flavours of the same statistic. They are different questions asked of different objects: one about the co-movement of returns over the short run, the other about the levels staying tied over the long run. Mix them up - measure correlation and assume you have found a reverting spread - and you have made the single most expensive error in the field.

Two definitions, kept honest

The picture above is the whole chapter in one frame. On the left, two prices that are highly correlated day to day but whose gap has no anchor: it widens and keeps widening. On the right, two prices that still wander like random walks, but with a spring between them - every time the gap stretches, something pulls it back to the mean. Only the right-hand gap is tradable. Correlation cannot tell these two pictures apart, because the daily co-movement looks identical in both. Cointegration is the test that can, because it asks the one question that distinguishes them: is the spread stationary?

Key idea

Correlation is necessary for a comfortable hedge but it is not a reversion signal. The reversion signal is cointegration - a stationary spread with a mean it returns to. A correlation screen finds pairs that trended together; only a cointegration test finds pairs whose gap comes back. Build your screen on the wrong one and you will systematically select pairs with no anchor.

The headline table: where the ranking breaks down

Enough theory. Here are both statistics computed directly on real NSE names over the window 2016-01-01 to 2026-06-25 - roughly 2,470 trading days per pair, each tested on its own overlapping history. Correlation is measured on log returns; cointegration is the Engle-Granger p-value on log price levels (small p means cointegrated - the null hypothesis is no cointegration). The table is sorted by correlation, so watch the verdict column as your eye travels down it.

Pair Sector Return corr Coint p Verdict
TATASTEEL / JSWSTEEL same 0.76 0.204 no - drifts
TCS / INFY same 0.67 0.062 borderline
HDFCBANK / ICICIBANK same 0.62 0.337 no - drifts
KOTAKBANK / HDFCBANK same 0.60 0.001 cointegrated
HCLTECH / WIPRO same 0.57 0.301 no - drifts
MARUTI / M&M same 0.53 0.000 cointegrated
MARUTI / ICICIBANK cross 0.46 0.277 no - drifts
TCS / HDFCBANK cross 0.26 0.388 no - drifts
INFY / TATASTEEL cross 0.26 0.842 no - drifts
WIPRO / MARUTI cross 0.24 0.791 no - drifts

Read the top row and the bottom of the same-sector block together, because that single comparison is the entire lesson. The most correlated pair in the table, TATASTEEL / JSWSTEEL at +0.76, is not cointegrated - its Engle-Granger p-value is 0.204, a flat "no, this spread drifts." Meanwhile MARUTI / M&M, with a much weaker +0.53 correlation, is firmly cointegrated at p = 0.000, and KOTAKBANK / HDFCBANK joins it at p = 0.001. A trader who ranked these pairs by correlation and traded the top of the list would have picked the steel pair - the one with no anchor - and skipped the two pairs that actually mean-revert. The correlation ranking and the cointegration ranking are simply not the same list.

In this window only 2 of the 6 same-sector pairs are cointegrated at the 5% level, and 0 of the 4 deliberately cross-sector "fake" pairs are - which is the one comforting result, because pairs with no economic reason to be tethered correctly fail the test. Now watch the difference on real prices: the most-correlated-but-not-cointegrated pair against a genuine one.

Top row, both pairs are rebased to 100 and both look like textbook "they move together." The difference is in the bottom row - the OLS residual spread as a z-score. TATASTEEL / JSWSTEEL wanders out to an extreme and stays there: there is nothing to trade, no level it returns to. MARUTI / M&M keeps crossing back through zero. That crossing - and only that crossing - is the edge.

Correlation tells you almost nothing about cointegration

One pair could be a fluke. So widen it. Below, the same two statistics over a 13-name set spanning IT, Banks, Metals and Auto: return correlation on the left, cointegration p-value on the right.

The correlation map on the left lights up in clean sector blocks - the IT names co-move, the banks co-move, the metals co-move, exactly as economic intuition says they should. The cointegration map on the right is patchy and stubbornly refuses to tile those same blocks. The bright (correlated) cells and the bright (cointegrated) cells do not line up. If correlation predicted cointegration, the two pictures would look the same. They look nothing alike, because they are measuring genuinely different things.

To turn "they look different" into a number, scatter every same-sector pair in the universe - correlation on the x-axis, cointegration p-value on the y-axis. If correlation predicted cointegration, the cloud would slope down to the right: higher correlation, lower p. It does not slope at all.

Across 75 same-sector pairs, only 11 are cointegrated at 5%, and the Spearman rank correlation between return correlation and the cointegration p-value is -0.04 (p = 0.75) - statistically indistinguishable from zero. Let that land. Knowing how correlated two stocks are tells you essentially nothing about whether their spread mean-reverts. The most popular screen in pairs trading - sort by correlation, trade the top - has almost zero predictive power for the only property that makes a pair tradable.

Tip

Use correlation for what it is good at and nothing more. It is a fine secondary filter once you have a cointegrated spread - a higher-correlation hedge has lower day-to-day variance and is more comfortable to hold. But it must never be your primary screen. Screen on cointegration first; rank the survivors on correlation, half-life and cost second.

Why correlation lures traders to their death

There is a reason correlation and regression are so seductive on price data, and it is worth seeing the machinery directly, because it is the engine under every spurious "pair."

Take two completely independent random walks - by construction there is no relationship whatsoever between them - and regress one on the other. Standard statistics says you should find significance about 5% of the time. On trending, non-stationary data you do not. You find it almost always. Here is the Monte Carlo over 3,000 such pairs.

Out of 3,000 pairs of independent random walks, the regression slope is "statistically significant" (|t| > 1.96) in 90.1% of cases - against the ~5% a correct test would deliver - with a median |t-statistic| of 10.0, and the R-squared exceeds 0.5 in 16.6% of runs on pure noise. This is the Granger-Newbold (1974) spurious-regression result, and it is the deep reason correlation is so dangerous on prices. Ordinary least squares assumes stationary, well-behaved inputs; feed it two trending series and it hands you a confident, completely fictitious relationship. A correlation or regression screen run on raw price levels is, quite literally, a machine for inventing pairs that were never tethered.

Heads up

A high t-statistic or a high R-squared on price levels is not evidence of a relationship - on trending data it is the default, even between things that have nothing to do with each other. Never trust a regression on non-stationary series. The only test that does not lie here is one that explicitly checks whether the residual spread is stationary: cointegration. That is precisely why the field runs cointegration tests instead of correlations.

The intuition, in plain words

Strip away the econometrics and it comes down to three sentences.

  • Correlation measures whether two names move together on the same days. It is a short-horizon, returns-level statistic. It can be coincidence, it drifts over time, and two strongly correlated prices can separate and never reconverge.
  • Cointegration measures whether a specific combination of the two prices stays tied to a mean over the long run - whether the spread is stationary. That mean is the magnet a pairs trade leans on. No stationary spread, no reversion, no trade.
  • Why the difference is lethal. A correlation-only screen selects pairs that trended together, which on non-stationary prices is mostly luck - the 90% from the histogram above. You enter expecting reversion, the spread keeps widening, and you average down into a position with no anchor. That is the classic blow-up, and it traces directly back to confusing these two ideas.

Where this breaks

The honest caveats matter as much as the result, because every number above is provisional.

  • Sample-window sensitivity is the big one. Every verdict in this chapter is true only in this window. Split each pair's history in half and re-run the identical test, and labels flip. MARUTI / M&M goes from p = 0.012 in the first half to p = 0.096 in the second - cointegrated, then no longer. INFY / HCLTECH swings from p = 0.007 to p = 0.861, a complete reversal. Cointegration is not a permanent property stamped on a pair; it is a statement about a particular stretch of data.

  • Multiple testing (preview of what's next). We scanned 75 same-sector pairs and 11 came back "cointegrated." With that many tests, a handful pass at p < 0.05 by chance alone - the same false-discovery trap the spurious-regression Monte Carlo warns about. A low p-value pulled from a big scan is a hypothesis, not a discovery; the next chapter puts numbers on how many spurious "cointegrated" pairs to expect from pure noise, and how Bonferroni and FDR control claw it back.

  • The Engle-Granger test is itself fragile. It assumes a single, constant hedge ratio over the whole window, it is sensitive to which leg you put on the left-hand side of the regression, and it loses power against structural breaks. Later chapters replace the static ratio with a residual-diagnosed and then dynamically updated hedge.

  • A tether is still not an edge. Even a genuinely, stably cointegrated pair only becomes tradable after costs, borrow and short availability, half-life, and out-of-sample survival. Correlation versus cointegration is the entry exam, not the finish line.

Real-life implementation note. Everything here uses real adjusted equity closes to learn the statistics. Trading a cointegrated spread means shorting one leg, which in Indian markets needs a real vehicle - intraday square-off, borrowed stock, or a stock-futures proxy - and each choice changes costs, margin, borrow availability, taxes and slippage. A statistical tether existing is not the same as a tradable edge existing. The gap between the two is the rest of this course.
