7.html:
The last chapter ended on a verdict: this pair is cointegrated, that one is not. That verdict is a single bit of information, and it is the least useful thing the machinery produces. The interesting questions all live downstream of it. How many units of the second stock hedge one unit of the first - and is that number even well-defined? What does the spread you would actually trade look like, and how do you prove it reverts rather than just hope it does? Once it reverts, how long does it take to come home, because that number is your holding period and your stop-loss horizon at once? This chapter opens the Engle-Granger engine and walks every moving part on one real cointegrated NSE pair, computing each number from the data and telling you, at each step, exactly where the number is lying to you.

The two-step machine, and a pair the data chooses

Engle-Granger is two steps and nothing more. Step one is an ordinary regression of one log price on the other; its slope is the hedge ratio and its residual is the spread. Step two tests that residual for stationarity. If the residual is stationary, the two prices are cointegrated and the residual is the asset you trade. Hold this picture, because every code cell below is one box in it.

We do not pick a pair by intuition. Fix one estimation window - 2019-01-01 to 2023-12-29, 1,239 trading days - hold it for the entire chapter so every number is comparable, and run the Engle-Granger coint test on all ten same-sector pairs among the five Nifty bank names. Holding the window fixed matters: cointegration is a statement about a particular stretch of data, and quietly re-selecting the window per pair is one of the easiest ways to fool yourself. In this window exactly one of the ten clears the 5% bar: HDFCBANK against KOTAKBANK, coint p = 0.0306 (t = -3.521 against a 5% MacKinnon critical value of -3.341). The runner-up, HDFCBANK against SBIN, sat at p = 0.096 - on the wrong side of the line, and an expert does not negotiate the threshold after seeing the number. That single survivor out of ten is your first warning about selection bias, which the next chapter attacks; for now we call HDFCBANK our A, KOTAKBANK our B. Everything works in log prices, because a slope on logs is a unit-free return-for-return relationship - exactly what cointegration theory and the half-life assume.

Note

The coint p-value of 0.0306 is already the honest number. It uses MacKinnon critical values that account for the hedge ratio having been estimated from the same data. Keep it in your pocket - in a moment a naive test on the same spread will hand you a far prettier p-value, and you need to know which one to believe.

Three hedge ratios, and why OLS lies

Step one's slope is the hedge ratio: how many units of B neutralise one unit of A. The obvious way to get it is sm.OLS(A, B). The trap is that OLS treats the right-hand variable as measured without error and minimises only the vertical distances to the line. But both stocks are noisy, so the answer changes depending on which one you put on the right.

Regress A on B and you get b = 1.0545. Regress B on A and you get b = 0.7859, which flipped back into A-on-B terms is 1.2724. If the hedge ratio were a clean physical constant, those two would be exact reciprocals and agree. They do not: they straddle the truth, 20.7% of the OLS value apart. The reason is exact and worth memorising - the product of the two slopes equals the regression R-squared: 1.0545 x 0.7859 = 0.8288 = R-squared. Since R-squared is below one, the two OLS slopes can never be reciprocal, and each is pulled toward zero. That is errors-in-variables, or attenuation, bias, and it is not a rounding artefact - it is a 21% disagreement about your position size.

The geometry tells you exactly when this matters. OLS A~B minimises the vertical scatter, implicitly assuming all the noise lives in A and B is pristine; OLS B~A assumes the opposite. Both legs are noisy stocks, so neither axis is the clean one, and the gap between the two slopes is governed entirely by R-squared - at R-squared = 1 the lines coincide, and as R-squared falls they fan apart until the choice of x dominates the answer. Our tight 0.83 makes the 21% gap the mild version; on a noisier pair the same mechanism can hand you two hedge ratios that differ by half, at which point your position is a directional bet wearing a spread's clothing.

Drawn on the joint scatter, the three lines are close only because the relationship is genuinely tight (R-squared = 0.83); on a looser pair the fan opens dramatically and your hedge ratio - and therefore your P&L - hinges on an arbitrary choice of which leg goes on the x-axis.

Total least squares: the honest middle line

The fix is to stop pretending either axis is clean. Total least squares - orthogonal distance regression, available directly as scipy.odr - minimises the perpendicular distance to the line, treating A and B symmetrically. The result does not depend on which series you called x, and it sits, by construction, between the two OLS slopes: 1.0545 <= 1.1751 <= 1.2724. The TLS hedge ratio of 1.1751 is 11.4% higher than the OLS A~B value we will actually use.

So which do we trade on? Engle-Granger is defined on the OLS residual, and with R-squared this high the OLS and TLS spreads behave almost identically, so the rest of this chapter builds the spread from OLS A~B to match the textbook two-step. The point of TLS is not that it is magic. It is that the hedge ratio is an estimate carrying a direction-of-regression ambiguity, and an expert states which convention they used instead of pretending the number fell from the sky.

Key idea

There is no single "correct" hedge ratio for two noisy series - there is a range, and you pick a convention. OLS A~B gives 1.0545, the flip gives 1.2724, TLS splits them at 1.1751. On a tight pair the choice barely matters; on a loose one it is the difference between a hedged spread and a directional bet wearing a spread's clothing.

Building the spread, and the formal test that it reverts

With the convention fixed, the spread is just the Step-1 residual: s = A - (alpha + beta.B). Subtracting the fitted beta.B strips out the shared sector and market trend, leaving a residual that - if the pair really is cointegrated - oscillates around a constant. OLS forces the mean to essentially zero (we measure -0.00000) with a standard deviation of 0.06982 in log units, and the series crosses its own mean 59 times in 1,239 days. Frequent mean-crossing is the visual fingerprint of reversion: a random walk would wander off and cross rarely.

Visual oscillation is suggestive, not proof. The formal test is the Augmented Dickey-Fuller test, which asks whether the spread has a unit root - a random walk - against the alternative that it is stationary. Here is the expert nuance that separates a careful researcher from an optimist. Run adfuller straight on the OLS residual and you get a t-statistic of -3.519 and p = 0.0075: a strong, confident rejection of the unit root. But that number is flattering, because the hedge ratio was fitted on the very data being tested - chosen, in effect, to make the residual as stationary as possible. The honest number is the Engle-Granger coint p of 0.0306 from the scan, which uses the correct MacKinnon critical values for an estimated cointegrating vector. Both clear 5%, so the verdict holds - but notice the gap. The naive ADF p (0.0075) is four times stronger than the honest p (0.0306) on the identical spread. Report the conservative one, always.

Heads up

The naive ADF p-value on a fitted residual is biased toward "stationary" by construction - you optimised the residual, then tested it. On a marginal pair this is exactly how a non-reverting spread gets waved through. When you have an estimated hedge ratio, the Engle-Granger coint p is the number that counts; the residual ADF is a diagnostic, not a verdict.

The spread's distribution is roughly bell-shaped and tight - skew -0.02, excess kurtosis -0.52 - which is what lets the standard-deviation bands later become entry and exit thresholds. Near-zero skew means it is not lopsided toward one stock; the slightly negative excess kurtosis means thinner tails than a normal, so the one- and two-sd bands actually mean what they say. A later chapter turns those exact lines into a rule - short the spread above the upper band, long below the lower one - and that rule only makes sense on a distribution this symmetric and tight. "Mean-reverting" should be something you can see, and here you can.

How fast it comes home: the OU half-life

Stationarity tells you the spread will return to its mean. It says nothing about when, and "when" is what you trade. Model the spread as an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process - the continuous-time spring that pulls a deviation back toward the mean - whose discrete version is an AR(1). Regress the daily change ds on the lagged level s(t-1); the slope is phi, the pull-back speed is kappa = -phi, and the half-life = ln(2) / kappa in trading days: the time to close half of any gap.

On our spread the AR(1) fit gives phi = -0.0237, so kappa = 0.0237, strongly significant (t = -3.83, p = 0.0001). Read kappa as a decay rate: roughly 2.4% of any gap closes per trading day, compounding to half the gap gone in ln(2) / 0.0237 = 29.3 trading days - about 1.4 months, just 2.4% of the sample. The significance matters as much as the estimate: a kappa indistinguishable from zero is no reversion at all, only noise leaning the right way - the t of -3.83 is what lets us call this reversion real rather than an artefact of the fit.

Read the half-life as a holding period: a typical deviation is half-gone in roughly six weeks, so a position is held days-to-weeks, not minutes and not years. That one number sets your trade horizon and your patience before you call a position broken rather than merely slow. A half-life that is a large fraction of the sample is a warning, not a signal - you have not seen enough complete cycles to trust the reversion is stable rather than a single lucky round trip.

The downward regression line is the mean reversion. Above-mean spreads tend to fall next; below-mean spreads tend to rise. A flat line would mean a random walk; an upward line would mean an explosive series running away from its mean.

The half-life mirage

Here is the trap that catches careful people. The half-life formula always returns a number - even when the spread does not revert at all. A finite, healthy-looking half-life is not evidence of anything on its own. The gates must be applied in order: first confirm the spread is stationary with the ADF test, then read the half-life. Skip the first gate and the second lies to you.

To prove it, contrast the real spread against three synthetic controls of the same length. A pure random walk reports kappa = 0.0025 and a half-life of 280 days - but its ADF p is 0.157, so it is not stationary and that half-life is meaningless. The explosive series (rho > 1) gives kappa = -0.0100, negative, so its half-life is simply undefined. And the dangerous one, the near-unit-root drift: kappa = 0.0108, positive, with a half-life of 64 days that looks perfectly tradeable - and its ADF p is 0.106, so it fails the stationarity gate outright. That 64-day half-life is a mirage. If you had trusted the half-life without testing stationarity first, you would have sized a position into a series that never comes home.

Standardised side by side, only the real HDFCBANK-KOTAKBANK spread keeps returning to its centre; the random walk and the near-unit-root drift wander off and stay there. The near-unit-root case is the one you meet in the wild - a pair that is almost cointegrated, drifting slowly enough that the half-life returns a plausible 64 days while the relationship is quietly broken. The ADF test is the only thing between you and that trap, and only if you run it before the half-life seduces you.

Heads up

A positive kappa and a short half-life are necessary, not sufficient. The near-unit-root control here had both - kappa 0.0108, half-life 64 days - and still failed stationarity at ADF p = 0.106. The order of operations is non-negotiable: stationarity gate first, speed gate second. A half-life read off a non-stationary series is a number with no meaning attached.

And there is one more crack even in the genuine pair. We estimated a single beta over the whole window, but a trailing 120-day OLS hedge ratio swings from -0.312 to 1.297 - a range of 258% of its own mean of 0.624. The "constant" cointegrating vector is anything but, which is the entire motivation for the dynamic Kalman hedge ratio a later chapter builds: a fixed-beta spread fitted in-sample drifts out of its bands out-of-sample for reasons that have nothing to do with reversion.

Where this breaks

Every box in the Engle-Granger diagram is an assumption that can fail. In rough order of how often it kills a real pair:

  • Hedge-ratio instability is the big one. The rolling beta wandered across a 258%-of-mean range. Engle-Granger assumes one cointegrating vector for all time; when beta drifts, the fixed-beta spread is mis-hedged, leaks directional risk, and breaches its bands for reasons unrelated to reversion. Rolling-OLS or Kalman can chase the drift, but every added degree of freedom is a fresh chance to overfit.
  • The x-versus-y ambiguity is real money. OLS A~B gave 1.0545, the flip gave 1.2724 - a 20.7% disagreement on a tight pair. On a looser pair it is far worse, and your P&L then depends on a modelling convention rather than on the market. TLS is more honest but still assumes a single constant ratio.
  • In-sample cointegration is an overfit waiting to happen. We scanned ten bank pairs and reported the one that passed at p = 0.0306. That is the best of ten tests dressed up as a single result - classic selection bias. The next chapter puts numbers on how many spurious "cointegrated" pairs pure noise manufactures once you scan a universe.
  • The half-life is fragile out-of-sample. The 29.3-day figure is an in-sample point estimate with wide error bars. A value that looks tradeable in this window can lengthen, vanish, or go negative in the next - and a spread whose half-life blows up is a position that simply does not come back. Re-estimate out-of-sample, every time.
  • ADF on a fitted residual flatters you. The naive ADF p (0.0075) was four times stronger than the honest coint p (0.0306) on the identical spread, precisely because the residual was optimised. Report the conservative number.
  • Stationary in logs is not tradeable in rupees. This entire chapter lives in adjusted log prices. Costs, the bid-ask crossed on two legs, borrow for the short, and the gap between adjusted and tradable prices are all still ahead of us. Treat the short leg as a research abstraction until you have a real implementation path - in Indian markets that may mean stock futures, borrowed stock, or intraday square-off, and each choice changes costs, margin, borrow availability, taxes, slippage and risk. A statistical relationship existing is not the same as a tradable edge existing.

The next chapter confronts the selection-bias problem this one walked into on purpose: how to scan a whole universe for cointegrated pairs without manufacturing false discoveries out of noise.

8.html:
A cointegration p-value of 0.03 feels like a discovery. Run it once, on a pair you had a genuine economic reason to test, and it is one. Run it across five hundred pairs and keep the ones below 0.05, and you have not built a watchlist - you have built a machine for manufacturing false positives. The test never changed. The number of tests did. This chapter is about searching a universe for tradable pairs without lying to yourself along the way, and the punchline on real NSE data over this window is bleak: a disciplined funnel that starts with 73 candidate pairs ends with zero that survive every honest gate. Far better to learn why here than to fund the lesson with a trading account.

Start with a reason, not a scan

The original sin of pairs research is the blind scan. The universe here is 50 NSE names, and with 50 names there are C(50, 2) = 1,225 possible pairs. Point a cointegration test at all of them, keep everything under p < 0.05, and pure chance alone hands you roughly 0.05 x 1,225 = 61 "cointegrated" pairs that mean absolutely nothing - and you have no way to tell those 61 ghosts apart from anything real. You have not screened a universe; you have taken 1,225 lottery tickets and circled the winners after the draw.

The fix is an economic prior: only test pairs that have a structural reason to share a long-run driver. Two private banks, two IT-services exporters, two steelmakers - names exposed to the same demand, the same input costs, the same regulation and rate cycle - are at least plausibly tethered. Restricting the scan to within-sector pairs is not merely good economics; it is good statistics, because it collapses the number of tests from 1,225 to 78 same-sector candidates (6.4% of the blind universe). That single decision drops the expected pure-chance "winners" from about 61 to about 4. Nothing else in this chapter moves the false-discovery problem as hard as choosing what not to test.

Key idea

The biggest lever on false discovery is not the correction you apply at the end - it is the size of the search you start with. A same-sector prior shrinks the test count roughly 16-fold (1,225 -> 78) and the expected pure-chance winners from ~61 to ~4 before a single test runs. Choosing what not to test is the cheapest risk control in the field.

The scan: 16 of 73 "pass"

Two honesty rules go in before any scan. First, the scan and the verdict use different data. We fix an in-sample window (2018-01-01 to 2023-12-31) on which we scan and correct, and hold back an out-of-sample window (2024-01-01 to today) the scan never touches. If a pair only looks tethered because we peeked at its whole history, the held-back window will catch it. Second, thin histories are dropped, not stretched: recent listings and renames lack a full in-sample window, and a cointegration test on a short overlap is noise, so we require a minimum number of in-sample rows. That requirement drops 5 of the 78 candidates, leaving 73 pairs with enough history to test.

The test itself is Engle-Granger on log price levels, where the null hypothesis is no cointegration, so a small p-value means tethered. Run it across all 73 and the heatmap below shows the result sector by sector - colour reversed so that bright cells are the low p-values.

Notice how few cells light up even within a sector. Belonging to the same industry buys you a sensible hypothesis; it never buys you a guaranteed tether. Keep every pair with p < 0.05 and the naive scan hands you 16 of the 73 as "cointegrated." Sixteen pairs. That is what an undisciplined screen would print, frame as a watchlist, and start sizing. Hold that number in your head, because the next stage asks the only question that matters: how many of those 16 would you have found in pure noise?

How many would pure noise hand you?

Here is the uncomfortable experiment. Generate the same number of independent random-walk pairs we just tested - by construction with no relationship whatsoever - and scan those for cointegration. Because the Engle-Granger test is calibrated to a 5% significance level, roughly 5% of these noise pairs will "pass" at p < 0.05 by sheer construction. We drew 6,000 independent random-walk pairs (seeded, each of length T = 1,484 to match the typical in-sample sample size) and measured the false-positive rate directly: 5.25%, almost exactly the nominal 5% the test promises. Multiply that by our 73 tests and the expected number of spurious "cointegrated" pairs from pure noise is about 3.8.

So the real scan found 16, of which roughly 4 are what a scan of this size would have produced from nothing - leaving about 12 above the noise floor. That gap, not the raw count, is the only part that could be real. Put bluntly: a quarter of the watchlist a naive screen just handed you is statistically indistinguishable from coin flips. The chart below puts the real scan beside the full distribution of false positives from pure noise.

Heads up

A low p-value pulled from a large scan is a hypothesis, not a discovery. At a 5% threshold the test is designed to fire on about one in twenty unrelated pairs, so the more pairs you test, the more guaranteed-meaningless passes you collect. Never quote a single pair's p-value without also stating how many pairs you tested to find it. The p-value of the winner is conditioned on the size of the search.

Clawing it back: Bonferroni and BH-FDR

Two standard corrections turn "I ran one test" mathematics into "I ran 73 tests" mathematics, and they trade off boldness against safety very differently.

Bonferroni controls the chance of even one false positive across the whole family of tests. The rule is brutal in its simplicity: only believe a pair whose p-value is below alpha / m, here 0.05 / 73 = 0.00068. With dozens of tests that threshold is tiny, so Bonferroni is conservative by design - it will throw out genuine pairs to be certain it admits no junk. In this window exactly 1 of the 16 survives it.

Benjamini-Hochberg (BH) FDR is gentler. Instead of forbidding every false positive, it controls the expected fraction of false discoveries among the pairs you keep. Sort the p-values ascending; the largest rank i where p(i) <= (i / m) x alpha sets the cutoff, and you reject everything at or below it. BH keeps more pairs than Bonferroni while still bounding the junk rate - here the cutoff lands at p <= 0.0048 and 7 pairs survive.

Note

Bonferroni answers "how do I avoid any false positive?" and BH answers "how do I keep the false-positive fraction small while still finding things?" Neither is universally correct. Bonferroni suits a setting where one bad pair is catastrophic; BH suits a research pipeline where a controlled minority of duds is acceptable because every survivor faces a further out-of-sample gate. We use BH precisely because that next gate exists.

The arithmetic is sobering on its own. Of the 16 pairs the naive screen called discoveries, 9 were demoted the moment we accounted for the fact that we ran 73 tests rather than one. They were never findings; they were the price of the scan.

The only referee that counts: out-of-sample

Everything so far happened on the in-sample window - the data we mined. A pair that survives correction in-sample is, at best, a strong hypothesis. It is not a result, because we chose the window, the universe and the sector map specifically to surface it. The honest referee is data the scan never saw: re-run cointegration on the held-back 2024-to-today window and keep only the pairs that hold up there too.

Brace for it. Of the 7 BH-FDR survivors, the number that remain cointegrated at p < 0.05 on the out-of-sample window is zero. Not one. The funnel that began with 73 candidates, narrowed to 16, and was corrected down to 7, collapses to 0 out-of-sample-confirmed pairs in this window. The disciplined search, run honestly end to end, finds nothing it can stand behind.

That zero is not a bug in the method; it is the method working. The whole apparatus - the prior, the Monte Carlo, the two corrections, the held-back window - exists to make exactly this outcome visible before capital is committed, rather than after. An undisciplined screen would have stopped at the 16-pair bar, declared victory, and traded a watchlist with about 4 pure-noise pairs baked into it and no out-of-sample evidence for any of them.

Tip

Fix every rule of the search - window, universe, sector map, minimum history, correction, out-of-sample split - before you look at a single result, and then report the entire funnel, not just the bottom bar. The corrections control error given the tests you ran; they cannot undo a search that was redesigned until it produced winners. "Zero survivors this window" is a legitimate, publishable answer. Forcing a non-zero answer is how the fooling starts.

The honest workflow

Compress the chapter to one line and it reads: economic prior -> scan on a common in-sample window -> correct for multiple testing -> confirm out-of-sample -> only then consider costs and tradability.

Skip the prior and you scan 1,225 pairs and drown in roughly 61 false positives. Skip the correction and you trade noise that "passed" at p < 0.05 only because you ran 73 tests. Skip out-of-sample and you trade a relationship you fitted to history and never challenged. Each gate exists because the gate before it is not enough. And a pair that clears all four is still only a candidate - it has earned the right to be cost-tested, nothing more. The window numbers will differ on a different split, but the shape never does: the funnel always narrows, usually far more brutally than the top bar promised.

Where this breaks

  • Selection bias dwarfs the p-value. Even after Bonferroni and BH, the pairs you keep are the best of a search you designed. The corrections control error given the set of tests you ran, not the human choices that built that set - the window, the universe, the sector map, the minimum-history cut. Tune any of those and a different set of "survivors" appears. The only defence is fixing the rules before looking and reporting the whole funnel.
  • The universe is a survivor-only set. These are today's index constituents. Names that were dropped, merged or delisted never enter the scan, which flatters every survival rate here. A true point-in-time universe carrying its dead names would lower the hit rate further.
  • One out-of-sample window is one draw. A single 2024-to-today window is a single referee. A pair can confirm here and die next quarter, or fail here on a two-year wobble and be perfectly tethered over a decade. One out-of-sample pass is evidence, not proof; walk-forward re-tests this many times and the survival rate keeps falling.
  • The corrections assume the tests are roughly independent. Same-sector pairs share legs - one bank name appears in several bank pairs - so the p-values are correlated. Bonferroni is then too harsh and BH's guarantee becomes approximate. The direction of the lesson holds, but the exact survivor count is softer than the clean integers suggest.
  • A surviving tether is still not an edge. Nothing here touched the bid-ask, the transaction costs, the short-leg vehicle, the half-life, or the capacity. Passing this funnel earns a pair the right to be cost-tested. In Indian markets the short leg needs a real vehicle - an intraday cash short, a borrowed-stock arrangement, or a stock-futures proxy - each with its own cost, margin and availability. Finding a pair without fooling yourself is the cheap part; everything the rest of this course adds on top is the expensive part.

9.html:
Here is a backtest that returns +98.2% gross, posts a 0.99 Sharpe, wins 72.5% of its trades, and never once hits its stop. It trades a real NSE pair on real adjusted closes, with honest next-bar fills and no obvious bug. By the end of this chapter you will have built it line by line and watched it work. And you should not believe a single number in it. This is the most seductive chapter in the course, because everything in it is true and almost none of it survives. The whole point is to assemble a gorgeous in-sample curve on purpose - cleanly, correctly, every figure computed in this data window - so the next chapter can take it apart and you can feel, in your own backtest, exactly how a good-looking result fools the person who built it.

The assembly line: two prices in, one position out

A pairs trade is a short factory line. Prices go in; a hedge ratio collapses two of them into one spread; a rolling z-score measures how stretched that spread is right now; thresholds turn the z-score into a position; and - the box most backtests quietly skip - the position is filled on the next bar, never the same one. Every section below is one box in this diagram.

The pair is HDFCBANK against KOTAKBANK, two large private banks, chosen because earlier chapters tested it cointegrated and measured its half-life. A hedge ratio fitted on data that includes the future is a magic trick, not a strategy, so we split time: estimate everything - the hedge ratio, the spread, its statistics - on a training window of 2019-01-01 to 2023-12-29 (1,239 trading days), and grade it only inside that window for now. The genuine out-of-sample test is the next chapter.

First, re-verify the pair is cointegrated on the training window itself, because if it were not, there would be nothing to trade. Engle-Granger returns t = -3.521, p = 0.0306 - cointegrated at the 5% level, in this window. The Step-1 regression A = alpha + beta*B gives a hedge ratio beta = 1.0545 (R-squared 0.83), and the residual is the spread, s = A - alpha - beta*B, in log units. Reading: roughly one unit of HDFCBANK exposure is hedged by about 1.05 units of KOTAKBANK exposure - in this fit.

The spread is not the price ratio

A great deal of "pairs trading" online watches the price ratio, P_A / P_B, and trades when it stretches. That silently assumes the correct hedge is one rupee of A against one rupee of B - a beta of exactly 1 in log space. Cointegration disagrees: it estimates beta, and the ratio is only the special case where beta happens to equal 1. The test of which one is real is stationarity, so we ADF-test both on the training window.

The log price ratio (the implied beta=1 spread) tests at ADF p = 0.0138; the estimated cointegrating spread tests at ADF p = 0.0075 - lower p, stronger reversion. Here beta is so close to 1 that the two lines nearly coincide, which is exactly why this is a safe pair to learn on. But do not generalise from how close they look. For a pair whose estimated beta is far from 1, the ratio can wander off and never come back (non-stationary) while the beta-weighted spread reverts cleanly. Letting the ratio choose your hedge is letting a coin flip choose it.

Key idea

The thing that mean-reverts is the estimated cointegrating spread, A - beta*B, not the raw price ratio. The ratio is a guess that beta = 1. Sometimes that guess is harmless (as here); sometimes it quietly turns a tradable spread into an untradable drift. Always trade the spread the data fitted, never the ratio the internet assumed.

The z-score, and why the window is 29 days

The spread's units - log price - mean nothing to a trader. The z-score rescales it to "how many standard deviations from its local mean," which is directly comparable to a threshold like 2 or 3. Two choices decide everything about how it behaves.

The first is static versus rolling. A static, full-window mean and standard deviation use information from the whole sample, including the future, so they leak look-ahead into every early bar. We use a rolling, trailing-only mean and standard deviation. It adapts to slow drift in the spread at the cost of being noisier - an honest trade we are willing to make.

The second is how wide a window. Too short and the z-score is jumpy; too long and it cannot react. The natural timescale is the spread's half-life - the time it takes to forget half of any deviation. Earlier work measured it at 29.3 days, so the rolling window is set to 29 days: we estimate the spread's local centre and dispersion over roughly one half-life. The window is not a free parameter we tuned; it is read off the spread's own physics.

We also build a robust z-score from the rolling median and MAD (median absolute deviation, scaled by 1.4826 to match a normal standard deviation). A single jump wrecks a mean and a standard deviation; the median and MAD shrug it off - and spreads gap.

The two z-scores correlate at 0.948 - close, but not identical. The ordinary z has mean +0.06 and standard deviation 1.30; the robust z has mean +0.11 and standard deviation 1.57, running a touch hotter precisely because it refuses to let outliers inflate its dispersion estimate. Through a clean stretch they agree; through a gap, the robust version stays calmer and fires fewer panicked signals.

Tip

Default to the robust (median/MAD) z-score for any spread that gaps - earnings, news, a corporate action on one leg. It costs one line of code and it is the difference between a measured signal and one yanked around by a single bad print. Keep the ordinary z as the sanity check; when the two diverge sharply, that divergence is itself a warning that the spread just did something violent.

Four zones: enter, exit, stop

The rules are deliberately simple and symmetric. Enter when 2 < |z| < 3.5: stretched, but not broken. If z is above +2 the spread is rich, so short the spread (short A, long B); if z is below -2 it is cheap, so long the spread (long A, short B). Exit when |z| < 0.5: the spread has come home, bank it. Hard stop when |z| > 3.5: a move this large usually means the relationship is breaking, not stretching, so get flat and do not re-enter until z is back inside the band. The z-axis therefore splits into four zones.

Running these rules over the training window decides a position at every close: 262 long-spread bars, 302 short-spread bars, 675 flat bars - and zero hard stops triggered in this window. That last number should make you suspicious, not happy. A strategy that never once hit its stop in five years did not find a magically safe spread; it found a stretch of history kind enough never to push the relationship past breaking. The next window may not be so polite.

Next-bar fills and rupee-neutral sizing

The single most common way a pairs backtest lies is the same-bar fill: computing a signal from today's close and then pretending you also traded at today's close. You cannot - that close has already happened by the time you know the signal. So the rule, stated and enforced: the signal is computed at the close of bar t, the position goes on at the close of bar t+1, and P&L accrues only from t+1 onward. In code it is a single .shift(1), and it is the whole difference between an honest curve and a fantasy.

Now the sizing. The signal comes from the beta-weighted spread, but how big is each leg? We size the two legs to equal rupee notional - one rupee long, one rupee short - so the book is cash-neutral and, to first order, market-neutral. The position is therefore +1 (long spread: +1 rupee A, -1 rupee B), -1 (short spread), or 0 (flat), and the per-bar P&L on a +1 book is simply r_A - r_B. With beta at 1.05 here, equal-rupee sizing and exact beta-weighted sizing are almost the same thing; for a pair with beta far from 1 they are not, and the difference leaks back in as directional market exposure. Flagging that gap is the honest move, and we return to it below.

Note

The short leg in this backtest is a research abstraction. A long/short equity book assumes you can borrow and sell short one of these banks cheaply and hold it for an average of two weeks. In Indian markets that requires a real vehicle - intraday square-off, securities borrowing, or a stock-futures proxy - each with its own cost, margin and availability, none of which appears in an equity-close backtest. A statistical relationship existing is not the same as a tradable edge existing.

The curve that is meant to seduce you

Here is the whole strategy in one frame: the spread on top with its rolling mean and the +/-2 (enter) and +/-3.5 (stop) bands - all time-varying, because the z-score is rolling - and the z-score below with the four zones shaded and the actual entries (triangles) and exits (crosses) drawn on the executed bar, one bar after the signal fires.

You can read the entire logic off that picture: the spread stretches to a band, an entry triangle appears one bar later, the spread walks back toward its rolling mean, an exit cross lands inside the green zone. Now compound it.

One unit of the rupee-neutral book grows to +98.2% gross over the window, at an annualised Sharpe of 0.99, with a maximum drawdown of -18.8% and 46% time in market. Notice the shape: the curve is flat whenever the book is flat and earns in bursts when a stretched spread snaps back. That is precisely what mean reversion should look like - which is part of what makes it so convincing. The trade ledger backs it up: 40 round trips, a 72.5% hit rate, +1.78% average trade, average winner +3.42% against average loser -2.54%, held about 14 days each.

The trade distribution is textbook mean reversion: a tight cluster of small wins, a thin left tail of larger losses, and holding periods scattered around the 29-day half-life - exactly why the z-score window was tied to it. By every conventional metric on the page, this is a strategy you would fund.

It is not. We even peeked at costs: one pair round trip - two legs, in and out - runs roughly 28 bps of leg notional, and 40 of them stack to about an 11% additive drag against the +98% gross. That alone does not kill it. But cost is the smallest of the problems, and stacking the rest is the entire job of the next chapter.

Heads up

Believe none of these numbers as a forecast. Every parameter - the hedge ratio beta, the half-life, the z-score window, the three thresholds - was fitted or chosen inside this window, and a model graded on its own training data always passes. The next chapter takes this exact pair, this exact beta and these exact rules out-of-sample, subtracts realistic costs, and watches the Sharpe collapse. The pretty curve above is built to be demolished. Read it as a warning about your own future backtests, not as an edge.

Where this breaks

This curve is designed to look good, and it stands on five soft spots the next chapter presses until they crack.

  • It is in-sample. The beta, the half-life, the window and the thresholds were all fitted or chosen inside 2019-2023. The only honest grade is out-of-sample, and we have not run it yet. Expect most of the +98% and most of the 0.99 Sharpe to be a property of the training data, not the strategy.
  • It is gross of costs. Every round trip crosses the bid-ask on two legs, twice, and pays the full statutory stack. The back-of-envelope drag is ~11% here; the honest net curve, with slippage and impact, is heavier and comes later.
  • Beta is frozen, but it drifts. We froze a single in-sample hedge ratio. Out-of-sample it will be the wrong number, the spread will mis-hedge, and bands will be breached for reasons that have nothing to do with reversion. Later chapters make beta time-varying.
  • Rupee-neutral is not exactly the cointegrating hedge. Equal-rupee sizing equals the beta-weighted hedge only because beta is near 1 in this window. For a different pair the residual market exposure leaks in as directional P&L that has nothing to do with the spread.
  • The short leg is a research abstraction. The whole book assumes cheap, reliable shorting of a single stock for weeks at a time. In Indian markets that is intraday cover, borrowed stock or a futures proxy - each with costs, margin and availability absent from this equity-price backtest.

Zero hard stops, a 72% hit rate and a near-doubling of capital are not evidence the strategy works. They are evidence the window was kind and the grader was the strategy's own teacher. Everything here is true, in this data window, gross, in-sample - and that sentence is the trap the next chapter springs.

10.html:
Here is the curve that gets a strategy funded. Take the HDFCBANK / KOTAKBANK pairs trade the previous chapter built - the same frozen hedge ratio, the same z-score, enter at plus-or-minus two standard deviations, exit at the mean, hard-stop at four - run it over 2019 to 2023, and it prints a net Sharpe of 1.01, a total return of +110.5%, and drawdowns shallow enough to sleep through. Twenty-two clean round trips. It looks like money. This chapter takes that exact curve and dismantles it the way a risk committee would before a single rupee is committed, because a backtest is a hypothesis wearing the costume of a result. Between the pretty chart and a real profit-and-loss sit four leaks, and we are going to open each one on the same pair, with the real numbers, in this data window.

Four leaks between a chart and a P&L

Hold one picture for the whole chapter: the top line is the kind of curve a naive backtest prints, the bottom line is what you could actually have kept, and every gap between them is one of four leaks - not edge. The rest of the chapter is just measuring each gap on real data.

Two honesty rules are baked in before we start, so we are not fooling ourselves on the first page. The hedge ratio b = 1.0545 is estimated on the train window only and then frozen - the test period never touches it. The z-score is trailing (a rolling 60-day window, about two half-lives), so the signal at any point uses only past data. The fill is next-bar: the signal forms at today's close and the trade is executed on the next bar. Costs are delivery (CNC) costs - a round trip is 0.474% of notional - because a roughly 29-day half-life means positions are held for weeks, not squared off intraday. With those rules fixed, we open the leaks.

Teardown one: the edge does not survive a blind window

The single most important question about any backtest is whether the rule works on data it was not built on. We froze the hedge ratio and the thresholds on 2019 to 2023 and applied them, completely unchanged, to 2024 to now - data the model has never seen.

The in-sample net Sharpe of 1.01 falls to 0.34 out of sample - about two-thirds of the edge gone - leaving +11.5% over two and a half years across just 11 round trips, with a -13.8% drawdown. The shape of the curve changes too: the smooth in-sample climb becomes a sideways grind that happens to end up. That is not a strategy that "still works a bit"; it is a strategy whose headline number was mostly an artefact of the window it was tuned on.

A fairer test re-estimates the hedge ratio every quarter on the trailing two years and trades the next quarter forward - still strictly out-of-sample, but allowed to keep up with drift. That walk-forward lands at Sharpe 0.62 overall and +22.3% across 2024 onward (the only genuinely recent slice). It recovers some of the loss, but it sits far below 1.01, and every refit is one more chance to overfit. There is no free lunch in re-estimation.

Heads up

The in-sample curve was a hypothesis, not a result. Held out blind, the net Sharpe fell by roughly 66% in this window. Any single backtested Sharpe you are shown without an out-of-sample number beside it should be treated as the optimistic end of a range, not the answer.

Teardown two: costs are not a footnote

A pair round trip is not one trade - it is eight fills: two legs, each bought and sold, on entry and on exit. Every fill pays statutory charges (securities transaction tax, exchange and regulator fees, GST, stamp duty), plus half the bid-ask spread it crosses, plus its own market impact. For a multi-week delivery hold the dominant term is the 0.1% transaction tax on the sell side - there is no cheap intraday square-off to dodge it.

Over the full sample that is 33 round trips times 0.474% = 15.9% of capital paid away as costs. Gross Sharpe 0.90 becomes net 0.77; total return +175% becomes +135%. In this window costs are a haircut, not a guillotine - precisely because a plus-or-minus-two-standard-deviation rule trades rarely. But that verdict depends entirely on the cost you assume, and the half-spread and impact are the two numbers you can least defend in advance: they move with your size, the time of day, and how the order book looks when you actually trade.

So instead of trusting one number, sweep it. As the assumed round-trip cost rises, the out-of-sample net Sharpe reaches zero at about 1.9% round trip - and the out-of-sample line sits much closer to that zero axis than the in-sample line the whole way. The edge that has a comfortable cost cushion in-sample does not have the same cushion once it is out of sample. Your break-even is not a distant cliff; it is roughly four times the cost you hope to pay, and a bad fortnight of fills can spend that margin.

Note

The cost verdict is a function of an assumption you cannot pin down before trading. When a strategy's sign flips somewhere between a 0.5% and a 2% round-trip cost, "is it profitable?" is the wrong question. The right one is "how confident am I in the 0.474%, and what does the answer look like if I am wrong by a factor of two?"

Teardown three: the relationship was never stable

Teardown one showed the edge fades out of sample. This is why. The whole strategy assumes one stable cointegrating relationship - a single hedge ratio, a spread that always comes home. We test that assumption directly with a rolling Engle-Granger p-value: is the pair cointegrated right now?

The rolling p-value runs from a min of 0.005 to a max of 0.978, and it sits at 0.734 in the latest window. The pair fails the 5% cointegration test in about 90% of rolling windows. Reversion is the exception for this pair, not the rule - the clean, tradeable cointegration the build chapter found was one lucky stretch of data, not a permanent property of the two names.

The breakdown has a mechanical cause: the hedge ratio is not a constant. The rolling 120-day b swings from -0.312 to 1.744, a mean of 0.649 with a standard deviation of 0.417 - it wanders by 317% of its own mean and even turns negative, which means the "hedge" briefly inverts and both legs point the same way. The frozen train value of 1.0545 sits about +63% above the test-period average. Out of sample the spread we trade is therefore mis-hedged by construction: it carries directional bank-sector risk that has nothing to do with mean reversion, and that leaked directional risk is exactly what shows up as the out-of-sample drawdowns.

Key idea

De-cointegration is not a separate problem from the out-of-sample collapse - it is the engine of it. A fixed hedge ratio trades a snapshot of a regime that has already ended. When the spread fails the cointegration test in nine windows out of ten, you are not trading a tether that occasionally stretches; you are trading a directional bet that occasionally happens to revert.

Teardown four: leaks that flatter quietly

The first three leaks are about the future being unknowable. The fourth is about the future quietly leaking into the past inside your own code - and about the pairs you never see. The most dangerous version is a normalisation leak: standardising the spread with the whole sample's mean and standard deviation instead of a trailing window. It looks like innocent scaling, but the scaling factor secretly contains the test period's own range, so the held-out test is no longer blind.

Switching the trailing z-score for a full-sample one lifts the out-of-sample net Sharpe from 0.34 to 1.12 - a 3.3x boost from a single subtle leak - and not a rupee of it is tradeable, because you cannot standardise today by statistics you only learn next year. The fill assumption is just as decisive: a same-bar fill returns -64% (Sharpe -0.70) against the next-bar +135% (Sharpe 0.77). Note the direction - for a mean-reversion entry the same-bar "cheat" is actually worse, because it books the very move that triggered the entry against you. The lesson is not that look-ahead always flatters; it is that a one-day shift in an assumption moves the result by more than the entire edge, in either direction.

Then there is the pair you are looking at. We did not stumble onto HDFCBANK / KOTAKBANK - the build process scanned ten bank pairs and crowned the one that tested most cointegrated in 2019 to 2023. That is selection, and it has a price.

Our pair ranked first of ten in-sample; run the identical net strategy across all ten same-sector bank pairs and 5 of the 10 actually lose money net out of sample. We did not find a good pair so much as run a beauty contest in-sample and crown a winner whose crown is worth little once the window changes. And these are only the banks still in the index - the pairs that merged or delisted are not on the chart at all, which means even this humbling picture is the flattering version.

Heads up

A low p-value pulled from a scan of many pairs is a hypothesis, not a discovery. Scan ten pairs and the best one looks special by construction; scan a hundred and a few will pass any test by chance alone. The honest number is not the winner's in-sample Sharpe - it is how the whole cohort, winners and losers and the delisted ghosts, did out of sample.

The honest verdict

Put every view of the same strategy side by side and the story is plain. The seductive in-sample net Sharpe of 1.01 is the best case; every honest adjustment moves it toward zero.

View Sharpe Ann. vol Total Max DD
In-sample gross 1.14 16.4% +134.2% -20.5%
In-sample net 1.01 16.4% +110.5% -20.9%
Out-of-sample gross 0.46 17.8% +17.5% -13.8%
Out-of-sample net 0.34 17.8% +11.5% -13.8%
Walk-forward 2024+ net 0.62 15.1% +22.3% -13.3%

And the chart we are not allowed to hide - the one a brochure would crop out.

This is the net, out-of-sample equity curve with its drawdowns - the only curve that corresponds to money you could actually have kept. Over two and a half years it returned +11.5% at a Sharpe of 0.34, and even that is before a single rupee of borrow cost, financing, slippage beyond our estimate, or the reality that you cannot freely hold a short equity position for weeks. An honest researcher publishes this curve, not the seductive one at the top.

Where this breaks

The strategy did not fail because of a bug. It failed because a backtest measures how well a rule fit the past, while trading needs how well a rule survives the future, and the gap between those is everything we just measured.

  • The in-sample curve was a hypothesis. Held out blind, the net Sharpe fell from 1.01 to 0.34 in this window. Re-fitting the hedge ratio recovers part of that to 0.62, but lands far below the headline and adds its own overfitting risk.
  • Costs are not a footnote. Eight fills per round trip, dominated by delivery transaction tax, turned a comfortable gross 0.90 into a marginal net 0.77 - and the out-of-sample sign flips somewhere around a 1.9% round-trip cost you cannot pin down in advance.
  • The relationship was never stable. The pair failed the cointegration test in about 90% of rolling windows and the hedge ratio swung 317% of its mean, even going negative. A fixed spread trades a regime that has already ended.
  • Leaks flatter quietly. A one-day shift in the fill, or a full-sample normalisation, moved the result by more than the entire edge - in either direction - and we crowned the in-sample winner of ten survivors while the broken and delisted pairs stayed invisible.

The bottom line: naive pairs trading on HDFCBANK / KOTAKBANK, net of realistic NSE costs and out of sample, is at best marginal - a low single-digit Sharpe and a near-flat equity curve, before borrow, financing, slippage beyond our estimate, and the short-leg problem. A statistical relationship existed in one window. That is genuinely not the same thing as a tradable edge, and the difference between the two is the price of admission to everything that follows. This is not a counsel of despair; it is the standard every later technique - dynamic hedge ratios, baskets, cross-sectional neutrality, proper sizing, and validation that does not fool you - must clear before it earns a place in the book. Every one of them has to pass the same four leaks measured here. Educational content only, not investment advice.

11.html:
A static hedge ratio is a photograph of a relationship that refuses to sit still for the camera. You fit one number - the slope of HDFCBANK on KOTAKBANK, say - on five years of data, and from that moment you act as if it were a law of physics: 1.055 units of KOTAKBANK short for every unit of HDFCBANK long, in 2019 and still in 2026. But two banks are not a law of physics. Their relative sensitivity moves with rate cycles, loan books, index reweightings and sentiment, and the moment that slope drifts, your "hedged" spread quietly stops being hedged - it leaks directional risk and breaches its own bands for reasons that have nothing to do with mean reversion. The obvious fix is a hedge ratio that updates every day, and the Kalman filter is the elegant way to do it. This chapter builds one from scratch - and then, with the same real data, shows you that the elegant thing lost the backtest to the dumb flat number. That result is the whole point.

The number that pretends to hold still

Start with the diagnostic that exposes the lie. Instead of one OLS fit over the whole window, re-fit A = alpha + beta * B every single day on a trailing 120-day window using only past data, and watch beta. If it sits still, the static number is defensible. If it wanders, the static spread is structurally mis-hedged.

On HDFCBANK / KOTAKBANK over 2018-2026 - a pair the cointegration test actually picked in-sample at p = 0.0306, so this is a real relationship, not a fabricated one - the rolling beta is damning. The static in-sample fit hands you beta = 1.055. The 120-day rolling beta over the same series ranges from -0.312 to +1.744, with a mean of 0.625 and a standard deviation of 0.413. Its full swing is 329% of its own mean, and it spends 7.5% of all days negative. Negative. For two banks. A negative hedge ratio says you should hedge one lender by going long the other - economic nonsense for two names that rise and fall together. The static 1.055 is not a stable truth; it is the average of a quantity that genuinely moves.

But notice the trap on the right of that picture. The rolling beta confirms the static number is a fiction - and it also shows the naive fix is its own disease. A 120-day window is so jumpy it manufactures hedge ratios no risk desk would ever trade, including the negative ones. What we actually want is something smoother than rolling OLS but still adaptive - precisely the gap a Kalman filter is built to fill.

Key idea

The static beta is provably wrong on this pair - the trailing estimate swings across 329% of its mean and even goes negative. But "the static number is wrong" does not imply "any adaptive number is right." Rolling OLS over-reacts; the question is whether a smoother adaptive estimate earns its complexity. Hold that question - the backtest answers it, and not the way you would guess.

A hidden state, estimated one bar at a time

Think of the hedge relationship as a hidden state you never observe directly and that drifts slowly over time. You only ever see prices. The Kalman filter is the optimal linear machine for exactly this situation: estimate a hidden, slowly-moving state from noisy observations, updating one bar at a time and using only the past.

The state we track is the pair (intercept, hedge ratio), written x = [alpha, beta]. We assume no structural reason for it to move in any particular direction - only that it can wander - so the transition is a pure random walk, x_t = x_{t-1} + w, where w has covariance Q. The observation links today's price of A to the state and today's price of B: A_t = alpha_t + beta_t * B_t + v, with observation noise variance R. Then the filter runs two steps, forever:

The beautiful part is that the filter hands you the tradeable spread for free. The innovation e_t = A_t - (alpha + beta * B_t) - the part of today's A the filter did not expect from yesterday's state and today's B - is the dynamic spread. It uses only the past state plus today's B, so it is genuinely out-of-sample at every bar. The Kalman gain K decides how much of each surprise to believe, and it is governed entirely by two knobs.

We parameterise the process noise the Chan (2013) way, with a single dial delta: Q = delta / (1 - delta) * I. As delta -> 0, Q -> 0 and the state freezes into a static beta with extra steps; as delta grows, the state sprints and re-hedges on every wiggle. R is the skepticism dial - large R says "most of today's move is noise, barely update," small R says "lunge at every print." Crucially, they only ever matter as a ratio: large Q/R trusts new data, small Q/R trusts the prior.

Note

There is no market-given value for delta or R. The market does not tell you how fast beta is "really" allowed to drift. We pick them - and that act of picking is a fit. A static OLS beta has essentially zero discretionary knobs once you fix the window; the Kalman adds two continuous ones whose only ground truth is "whatever made the curve look good." Remember that the next time a dynamic-hedge backtest looks gorgeous.

We start with a deliberately ordinary choice, delta = 1e-4 and R = 1e-3, and stress-test it later.

Three betas on one axis

With those settings the filter produces a beta that is everything we hoped for as a description. Over the full series its standard deviation is 0.087 - smoother than rolling OLS by a factor of nearly five (rolling sd was 0.413) - it ranges only from 0.635 to 1.067, and it never goes negative. It tracks genuine drift without the rolling estimate's nervous breakdowns.

Look at where the lines sit. The static beta is pinned flat at 1.055, but the market spent most of the sample with a true ratio nearer 0.73 - the Kalman line tracks down toward it while the static line stays stubbornly high. As a picture of "what was the hedge ratio actually doing," the Kalman wins outright. The static line is a single in-sample number that is wrong about most of the rest of the series. If description were the goal, we would be done. It is not. The goal is P&L.

The dynamic spread you actually trade

We trade the Kalman spread with the identical z-score machinery the static-beta backtest uses: standardise by a trailing 60-day rolling mean and standard deviation (no look-ahead), enter when |z| exceeds 2.0, exit near 0.5. Keeping the z-score machinery identical means the only thing that changes between the two backtests is the spread itself - a clean, single-variable comparison.

The top panel shows the dynamic spread with the filter's own +/- sqrt(S) uncertainty band; the bottom shows the rolling z-score crossing the entry and exit thresholds. It looks tradeable. It crosses zero, it stretches to the bands, it comes back. Every visual instinct says this should work at least as well as the crude static spread. Now we let the cash flows vote.

The honest backtest: the simple beta wins

Same rules for both models, no cheating: signal at the close, fill at the next bar (positions shifted one day); the beta used to size day t is the beta known at t, lagged into the P&L; net subtracts a full round-trip cost on both legs every time the position changes, with the B-leg cost scaling by beta; and in-sample and out-of-sample are always reported separately, because the IS curve is a hypothesis and only the OOS curve is evidence.

Here is the table the whole chapter has been walking toward. Net of costs:

Model IS net Sharpe OOS net Sharpe Trades
Static beta +1.12 +0.76 41
Kalman beta -0.53 -1.50 78

The thing we spent two sections proving was "wrong" - the frozen 1.055 - earns a healthy +1.12 Sharpe in-sample and, the part that actually matters, survives out-of-sample at +0.76 net. The Kalman beta, with all its adaptive sophistication and its lovely smooth tracking, posts -0.53 in-sample and -1.50 out-of-sample, and trades nearly twice as often (78 versus 41) - paying costs on both legs each time to lock in a worse result. More flexibility did not buy more edge. It bought more ways to be wrong, and a bigger commission bill for the privilege.

Heads up

Adaptivity is not free. On this real, genuinely cointegrated bank pair, in this window, the static hedge ratio beat the Kalman hedge ratio by 1.65 Sharpe points net out-of-sample, while trading roughly half as often. The dynamic model was a strictly better description of the relationship and a strictly worse trade. Never assume the more sophisticated estimator wins the P&L just because it wins the chart.

The knobs are a fit, not a discovery

A defender will say we just picked bad knobs - tune delta and R and the Kalman will shine. So we tested that directly. Sweep delta across two orders of magnitude at fixed R and watch the net out-of-sample Sharpe.

The OOS net Sharpe wanders from -0.08 at the gentlest setting down to -1.50, with no stable plateau - a tiny change in a knob we invented flips the result by more than a full Sharpe point. And the punchline: no value of delta clears the static OOS bar of +0.76. The best the Kalman manages is roughly break-even at the very smallest delta - which is the filter quietly turning itself back into the static beta. The closer it gets to the static number, the better it does.

Then the full two-dimensional delta x R grid, because the personality is the ratio, not either knob alone:

Of the 20 cells in the grid, the number that beat the static OOS net Sharpe of +0.76 is exactly 0. Not one. The surface is a landscape of hills and pits, not a number - and picking the prettiest cell after seeing the out-of-sample result is curve-fitting, not edge. An honest forward test would have to fix delta and R before the OOS window exists, and any cell you fix on this evidence is a coin you watched land heads.

Tip

If you must run a dynamic hedge, freeze delta and R on in-sample data and never touch them again, then judge the model only on the OOS window. The moment you re-tune after peeking at out-of-sample performance, your "result" is the maximum of a noisy surface - guaranteed to disappoint forward. The static beta's one virtue is that it has nothing left to tune.

Where this breaks

The Kalman filter is a genuinely better description of a drifting relationship than a static line. Whether it is a better trade is a separate question, and here, on a real cointegrated pair, in this window, net of costs, the answer was no. The ways it breaks:

  • The tuning is a fit, full stop. delta and R have no market-given value. The grid swings the net OOS Sharpe across a wide range and sometimes flips its sign; any single reported number is one cell of an overfitting surface. The static beta has no such surface to get lost on.
  • More flexibility, more ways to overfit. Every degree of freedom the Kalman adds is a degree the in-sample optimiser can exploit. The price of adaptivity is a model that fits noise as eagerly as signal - and out-of-sample, noise does not repeat.
  • A free intercept eats the very signal you trade. Letting alpha_t drift means the filter can quietly absorb a real, persistent mis-pricing into a "new mean" instead of flagging it as a deviation to fade. The dynamic spread can look mean-reverting on the chart while the tradeable edge has been filtered away into the moving intercept.
  • The filter chases noise and pays for it twice. A larger delta re-hedges on day-to-day wiggle, churning the book (78 trades versus 41), paying costs on both legs each time, and often locking in a worse beta just as the relationship wanders back. The net curve sits well below the gross curve for a reason.
  • A dynamic hedge can still de-cointegrate. Tracking beta bar by bar does not guarantee the spread is stationary; an adaptive ratio can follow a relationship right out of its mean-reverting regime, leaving you with a smooth, well-tracked, non-reverting series that is worse than useless.

Real-life implementation note. Everything here uses real adjusted equity closes to learn the statistics. The short leg is a research abstraction - shorting one bank against the other in Indian markets needs a real vehicle (intraday square-off, borrowed stock, or a stock-futures proxy), and each choice changes costs, margin, borrow availability, taxes and slippage. A statistical relationship existing - even a Kalman-tracked one - is not the same as a tradable edge existing. On this pair, the most sophisticated estimator did not even win the research backtest, let alone the implementation one.

12.html:
A pair is a quiet confession that you only trust yourself to estimate one number. But five bank stocks do not move in pairs - they are all dragged around by a single shared force (rates, the credit cycle, the banking factor), and the cleanest mean-reverting object hiding inside a sector is usually a basket of three to five names, not a hand-picked twosome. The promise of the basket is real: pool the reversion across several legs and no single bad quarter can snap the whole spread. The cost is brutal and badly advertised - instead of one hedge ratio you now estimate an entire vector of weights, and a vector fitted to one window is a fragile, high-dimensional object that overfits in ways a pair never could. This chapter builds the basket honestly with the Johansen test, then tears it down with its own out-of-sample numbers.

Why one pair is leaving information on the table

Engle-Granger handles exactly two series: pick a dependent variable, regress, test the residual. That is a deliberate simplification, and it throws away structure. The five large-cap banks we will use here - HDFCBANK, ICICIBANK, KOTAKBANK, AXISBANK, SBIN - share one dominant common factor. A pair of any two of them captures only a slice of that shared cycle, and the moment one name de-couples (a bad result, a regulatory action on a single bank) the two-leg spread breaks with nothing to cushion it. A basket spreads the bet across more legs, so a single name leaving the relationship does proportionally less damage.

We fix one estimation window for the whole chapter, 2019-01-01 to 2023-12-29 (1,239 trading days), and work in log prices throughout - Johansen, like Engle-Granger, is defined on I(1) levels, and a vector on logs reads as a unit-free return-for-return relationship. Rebased to 100 at the window start, the five banks fan out and wander back: the cross-sectional spread of the rebased lines ranges from 0 to 137 index points across the window. That co-movement is the necessary visual precondition. Whether a fixed combination of them is pinned to a mean is the sufficient part, and only a test can answer it.

Key idea

A basket pools the mean-reversion bet across several names, so no single de-coupling breaks the whole spread. That is the entire upside. The entire downside is that you have traded one well-pinned hedge ratio for a vector of k-1 free weights estimated from the same fixed sample - and estimation error is where baskets go to die.

What the Johansen test actually asks

Engle-Granger needs a left-hand-side variable; with k names there is no natural one, and there may be more than one stationary combination living inside the prices. The Johansen procedure treats all k series symmetrically. It fits a vector error-correction model and asks: of the k possible directions through these prices, how many are stationary (mean-reverting) rather than random walks? That count is the cointegration rank r.

The eigenvectors that fall out of the procedure are the basket weights, ordered by how strongly each combination reverts. We run it with det_order=0 (a constant in the cointegrating relation, appropriate for drifting price levels) and one lag (k_ar_diff=1). Both are modelling decisions, not defaults handed down from nature.

Note

Change det_order or the lag count and the chosen rank can change. An expert states these choices out loud rather than letting a library pick them silently - because two analysts who disagree on the deterministic terms can get different rank verdicts on the same prices, and neither is "wrong."

Running the test: trace and max-eigenvalue disagree

Johansen returns two sequences of statistics, each compared against tabulated 95% critical values. The trace statistic tests H0: rank <= r against rank > r; you walk r up from zero and stop at the first rank you cannot reject. The maximum-eigenvalue statistic tests rank = r against rank = r+1, a sharper one-step-at-a-time test. On our basket, in this window, they do not agree.

H0 trace stat trace 95% reject? max-eig stat max-eig 95% reject?
rank <= 0 75.18 69.82 yes 29.61 33.88 no
rank <= 1 45.56 47.85 no 24.85 27.59 no
rank <= 2 20.72 29.80 no 14.61 21.13 no

The trace test clears its bar at rank <= 0 (75.18 > 69.82) and then fails at rank <= 1 (45.56 < 47.85), so it picks rank r = 1: one stationary basket exists. The max-eigenvalue test never clears even its first bar (29.61 < 33.88), so it picks rank r = 0: no stationary combination at all. The two textbook statistics, run on identical data, return opposite verdicts.

That disagreement is not a nuisance to be smoothed over - it is the finding. When trace and max-eigenvalue diverge, the cointegration is weak. The max-eigenvalue test is the more conservative of the two, and the one to trust more for trading, because it guards against reading too many relations into noise. The honest move is to report both, take the cautious view, and trade only the first eigenvector if you trade at all. A weak, contested relationship is exactly the kind that fails to survive costs and regime change - which the rest of the chapter then demonstrates with numbers.

The cointegrating vector is your basket

The first column of the eigenvector matrix is the strongest-reverting combination, and its entries are the portfolio weights. Sign and scale are arbitrary, so we normalise the first leg (HDFCBANK) to +1; the others then read as "per one unit long HDFCBANK, hold this much of each name."

Leg Weight (HDFCBANK pinned to +1) Side
HDFCBANK +1.000 long
ICICIBANK -0.166 short
KOTAKBANK -0.518 short
AXISBANK -0.115 short
SBIN -0.017 short

So the basket is: long HDFCBANK against a weighted short of the other four, with KOTAKBANK carrying most of the short side. The basket spread is just vector . log-prices - the weighted sum Johansen claims is stationary - and we z-score it against its in-window mean and standard deviation. It crosses its mean 71 times across the window, the busy signature of reversion, and the entry/exit bands at plus and minus one and two standard deviations are visible in the lower panel.

Formally, the ADF test on the basket spread gives a statistic of -4.285 and a p-value of 0.0005 (1%/5%/10% criticals -3.44 / -2.86 / -2.57), a clean rejection of the unit root, and the AR(1) half-life is 23.7 trading days - about 1.1 months, a sensible holding period. The lag-1 autocorrelation of 0.971 is exactly what a 24-day half-life implies. On paper, this is a textbook mean-reverting basket.

Heads up

That ADF p-value of 0.0005 is optimistic by construction. The Johansen vector was fitted to make this exact basket as stationary as possible, so testing the fitted spread for stationarity is grading your own homework. The un-cheated evidence that any relation exists is the trace test from the previous section - and that one was already weak and contested. Hold the half-life and the p-value loosely.

Now the honesty: the vector will not hold still

Everything above is in-sample. The single most dangerous assumption in basket trading is that the weight vector is a constant of nature. It is an estimate with k-1 free parameters, and it moves. Re-run Johansen on the first half and the second half of the same window and the weights do not line up - they lurch.

Leg full window 1st half 2nd half
ICICIBANK -0.166 +0.621 -6.912
KOTAKBANK -0.518 -1.250 -4.981
AXISBANK -0.115 +0.581 -0.713
SBIN -0.017 -0.797 +8.564

Three of the four free legs flip sign between the two halves. ICICIBANK goes from a long (+0.621) to a heavy short (-6.912); SBIN swings from -0.797 to +8.564. A sign flip is not a rounding wobble - it turns a long leg into a short one, which is catastrophic for any book holding the position. A block-bootstrap of the full-window vector confirms it is not bad luck: the bootstrap standard deviation over its own weight is 1.43 for ICICIBANK, 1.07 for AXISBANK, and 1.84 for SBIN. When that ratio exceeds one, the weight's sign is not even reliably estimated.

The cleanest demonstration of the damage is to take the vector fitted on 2019-2023 and apply it, unchanged, to 2024-2026 - data it never saw. A genuinely cointegrated basket keeps reverting around the same mean. This one does not: the out-of-sample spread drifts out of its bands and the ADF p-value collapses from 0.0005 in-sample to 0.5583 out-of-sample - from "firmly stationary" to "cannot reject a random walk."

This is structural, not unlucky. A k-leg vector has k-1 weights estimated from the same fixed sample, so more legs means more parameters means more places for noise to enter. And the bill is real too: a one-way fill costs roughly 6.9 bps in our intraday cost model, so a two-leg pair round-trips at about 28 bps while a five-leg basket round-trips at about 69 bps - cost scales linearly with legs, and a basket trips its threshold more often because any single name can drag the spread across a band.

The selection trap: 94% in-sample, 0% out-of-sample

The deepest trap is the one nobody warns about: we are tempted not just to test a basket but to choose the best one. Enumerate every sub-basket of 3, 4, and 5 of these banks - 16 combinations - rank them by in-sample stationarity, and the winners look spectacular. 94% of them test stationary in-sample at the 5% level. Then apply each winning vector, unchanged, to the out-of-sample window, and of those in-sample winners, 0% are still stationary out-of-sample. The in-sample and out-of-sample p-values carry a correlation of just +0.27 - close enough to nothing.

The strongest in-sample basket - the full five-name set, ADF p = 0.00047 - posts an out-of-sample p of 0.558. The points hug the left edge of the chart (great in-sample) and smear across the top (dead out-of-sample); the bottom-left "survives both" corner is essentially empty. Picking the basket that tests best is look-ahead bias wearing a lab coat. The in-sample p-value tells you almost nothing about the future.

Tip

The honest workflow inverts the leaderboard. Start with an economic prior - these are all banks for a reason, not because a scan blessed them. Pre-register the rank and the vector before looking at out-of-sample data. Prefer the fewest legs that capture the relationship, because every added leg adds estimation variance and cost while rarely adding signal. Confirm out-of-sample, always. Never trade the basket that merely won the in-sample scan.

Where this breaks

Baskets buy you diversification of the mean-reversion bet and surrender almost everything else to estimation error. In rough order of how often each one kills a real basket:

  • The cointegrating vector is unstable. Re-estimated on two halves of the same window, three of four free weights flipped sign. A fixed vector is a snapshot of one regime, not a law. Any real implementation must re-estimate continuously and swallow the turnover and overfitting that brings.
  • Estimation error grows with the legs. With k-1 weights from one sample, the bootstrap standard deviation of several weights rivalled their magnitude, so even the sign was in doubt. Past a point, more legs is more noise, not more signal.
  • Out-of-sample, the basket can simply stop reverting. The exact vector that gave an in-sample ADF p of 0.0005 drifted out of its bands and failed the test (p = 0.558) in the next window. In-sample stationarity is a weak hypothesis about the future.
  • Selection is look-ahead by another name. 94% of sub-baskets looked cointegrated in-sample and 0% survived out-of-sample, with the two p-values nearly uncorrelated. Picking "the basket that tests best" manufactures a result that does not exist.
  • Trace and max-eigenvalue disagreed on the rank. When the two Johansen statistics diverge - r = 1 versus r = 0 here - the cointegration is weak, and weak cointegration is exactly what fails to survive costs and regime change.
  • Every extra leg costs. Round-trip cost scales linearly with legs (about 28 bps for a pair, 69 bps for a five-name basket), and a basket trips its threshold more often. The basket must out-earn the pair by a wide margin just to break even, and after costs it usually does not.

A basket is the right idea - pool the reversion, neutralise the common factor - and a dangerous object - a high-dimensional estimate that overfits. Treat the Johansen vector as a fragile prior to be re-checked out-of-sample, use the fewest legs that capture the relationship, and never trade the basket that simply won the in-sample scan. The next logical step is to stop hand-picking one basket at all and instead neutralise market and sector returns across the whole universe, ranking the residuals - a cross-sectional, factor-neutral book.

Real-life implementation note. Everything here uses real equity price series to learn the statistics. Treat the short legs as a research abstraction unless you have a valid implementation path. In Indian markets a short may require stock futures, borrowed stock, intraday square-off, or another instrument, and each choice changes costs, margin, borrow availability, taxes, slippage, and risk. A statistical relationship existing is not the same as a tradable edge existing.
