13.html:
Every strategy in this course so far has traded one relationship at a time - a pair, a Kalman-tracked pair, a Johansen basket. They all share the same ceiling: a single spread can only absorb so much capital, and the day it de-cointegrates you are left holding nothing but the wreckage of a hedge ratio that stopped working. This chapter builds the version of statistical arbitrage that does not have that ceiling - the one that runs across an entire universe at once, market-neutral by construction, and is the literal workhorse of every equity market-neutral desk on earth. It is the most scalable idea in the book. It is also, in this window and universe, the cleanest demonstration of the course's central thesis: a statistical relationship existing is not the same thing as a tradeable edge existing. The gross signal here is real, clean, and genuinely market-neutral. The book just turns over so fast that realistic NSE costs do not dent the edge - they invert its sign.

Strip the market, keep the wiggle

The whole idea fits in one sentence: most of a stock's daily move is just the market (and its sector) moving, so strip that out, and what is left - the idiosyncratic residual - tends to over-react and snap back. The mechanics are a regression. Take any one name and regress its daily return on the NIFTY return; the slope is its beta, and the scatter around the fit line is the residual, the part of the day the market does not explain.

For AXISBANK the market alone explains R-squared of 51% of its daily variance, and the residual standard deviation is 1.48% per day against a raw 2.11%. Read that the right way round: when you hold a single stock long, you are mostly holding the market with a small idiosyncratic rider attached. A market-neutral book is only allowed to bet on the rider. Across the whole 50-name universe, removing the market strips out a median 31% of each stock's daily variance; also removing the same-sector mean residual lifts that to a median 61%. So roughly two-thirds of a typical stock's daily wiggle is market plus sector, and the residual book trades only the remaining third.

Two honesty rules are wired in from the start. The beta is a 120-day rolling estimate - the beta on day t uses only returns up to t, never the future. And the sector neutralisation subtracts the same-day cross-sectional sector mean, so two banks both jumping on a banking-policy headline do not manufacture a fake signal between them. The notebook's phrase is the right one: "optionally remove sector" is not optional in practice - it is where most of the clean edge lives.

From residual to a market-neutral book

The residual is the raw material; the signal is the negative of each name's last k-day cumulative residual, then demeaned across the universe. A stock whose residual has fallen hard over the last couple of days gets a high signal and becomes a buy candidate; one whose residual has run up gets a low signal and becomes a short. Demeaning - subtracting the cross-sectional average - is what makes the book dollar-neutral by construction: the longs and shorts have to balance.

Turning that signal into a book is three lines of arithmetic: rank the names each day, demean the ranks so longs and shorts offset, and scale so the gross book is 1 (about 0.5 long, 0.5 short). Because the weights sum to zero and the residuals are already beta-stripped, the resulting book is dollar-neutral and approximately market-neutral - the realised book beta in this window comes out at a mean of +0.006 with a standard deviation of 0.058, which is as close to zero as a real book gets. On a representative recent day the longs were the residual losers (BHARTIARTL, BEL, BAJAJ-AUTO, SUNPHARMA, ONGC, ETERNAL) and the shorts the residual winners (INDIGO, M&M, RELIANCE, MAXHEALTH, TRENT, ADANIENT). Nothing in that list is a directional call on the market; every name is a bet that its own residual over-stretched and will revert.

Key idea

This is the structural upgrade over pairs. A pair removes one stock's market exposure by hedging it against one other stock - fragile, low-capacity, and dead when the pair de-cointegrates. A cross-sectional book removes the market from every name with a regression and diversifies the residual bet across the whole universe. When one name's residual stops reverting, it is one of fifty, not the entire book.

The edge is real - the decile test says so

Equity curves can flatter a strategy, so before plotting any P&L, ask the cleaner question: does the signal actually sort forward returns? Each day, bucket every name into deciles by signal - decile 0 is the strongest short, decile 9 the strongest buy - and average each decile's next-day residual return.

It climbs, broadly, left to right, and the top-minus-bottom spread is +3.89 basis points per day gross. Note the shape the notebook is honest about: it is broadly monotonic with a noisy extreme decile, not the immaculate staircase a curve-fitted result would draw. That noise is the honest signature of a real but thin edge.

The factor model genuinely matters here - this is not a place where extra modelling is wasted polish. Removing only the market gives a gross Sharpe of 0.51; also removing the same-sector mean residual lifts it to 1.34 - the within-sector co-movement was adding noise to the ranking, and deleting it roughly doubles the clean edge. A gross Sharpe a little above 1.3 on a genuinely market-neutral book is exactly the low-volatility profile a desk is built to harvest. So we have a real signal that sorts returns and a real factor improvement that nearly doubles it. Everything to here is the good news.

Note

The gross result is not a fluke or a leak. The beta is rolling and backward-looking, the fill is next-day (the signal forms at today's close and the book is held over tomorrow), and the signal still sorts forward residuals with a positive top-minus-bottom spread. In this window and universe, factor-neutral residual reversion is a genuine phenomenon. Hold that thought for exactly one section.

Then you try to harvest it

Here is the entire argument of the chapter on one chart. The gross curve is that clean residual-reversion edge grinding upward with a shallow drawdown. Then we subtract realistic NSE costs.

The same signal, on the same days - the only thing that changes is whether you pay to trade it. The gross Sharpe of 1.34 becomes a net Sharpe of -1.99 under intraday (MIS) costs. Under overnight (CNC) costs, which carry the heavier delivery transaction tax, the curve does not merely roll over - it falls off a cliff. This is not a haircut. In the pairs chapter, costs turned a gross 0.90 into a net 0.77 - a trim, because a plus-or-minus-two-sigma rule trades rarely. Here, costs take a positive 1.34 and hand back a negative 1.99. The edge is not reduced; its sign is reversed. A strategy that made money before costs reliably loses money after them.

Heads up

A net Sharpe of -1.99 is not "marginal" or "needs work". It means that if you traded this exact signal and paid realistic costs, you would lose money with the same statistical reliability that the gross book made it. The cost line is not eating into the edge from the side; it is sitting on top of it, larger.

Turnover is the whole story

Why does a 1.34-Sharpe signal die this badly when a pairs trade survived? One number: the book turns over close to its entire gross value every single day, which annualises to roughly 235 times a year. The signal lives at a one-to-three-day horizon and flips constantly, so almost the whole book is replaced daily.

Now do the arithmetic the desk does. The gross edge is about 2.48 bps per day. The MIS cost drag, at the realised turnover, is about 6.45 bps per day. You are paying roughly two and a half times your edge to capture it. The red shaded region - costs above edge - is most of the chart.

The one lever that genuinely helps is trading less. Sweeping the lookback k from 1 to 20 days slows the signal and drops turnover from 1.34x to 0.31x per day - and the net Sharpe climbs from -3.83 at k=1 toward -0.16 at k=15. It climbs, but it never crosses zero. Even at the slowest, stalest setting, the residual edge is too thin to clear the toll. You cannot out-clever turnover with a better factor model, and you cannot out-wait it with a longer holding period. The cleanest way to see the verdict is to invert the question and ask how cheap you would have to be.

The breakeven per-side cost is 2.66 bps. Realistic intraday cost is 6.9 bps and overnight 11.9 bps - the cheapest you can plausibly trade is more than two and a half times the cost at which the strategy stops making money. That gap, not any modelling flaw, is the precise reason this is a research model and not a strategy you switch on.

Tip

When a strategy's breakeven cost sits at a fraction of the cost you can actually achieve, stop tuning the signal. No lookback, no factor, no risk model closes a gap of that size. The only things that can are structurally lower cost (institutional execution, internalisation, patient limit fills instead of crossing the spread) and a far larger, more liquid universe to dilute impact - levers that live outside the backtest entirely.

Where this breaks

This signal is real and it is uneconomic, and those two facts live together. The verdict for this window and universe: a clean, genuinely market-neutral residual-reversion edge with a gross Sharpe a little above 1.3 once the sector is removed, that is decisively unprofitable after realistic NSE costs in the form shown.

  • Turnover and costs dominate, full stop. The edge is a few bps per day; realistic per-side cost is several bps applied to ~1.0x turnover daily, so net inverts the sign from a gross Sharpe of 1.34 to a net of -1.99. The breakeven of 2.66 bps is a fraction of the 6.9-11.9 bps you can actually trade at. No factor-model polish fixes an edge smaller than its own toll.

  • Capacity is tiny and impact is assumed away. The cost model uses a flat 2 bps impact, but a residual-reversion book demands liquidity exactly when it is scarcest - it buys what just fell and shorts what just ran. Real impact rises with size, so the modest gross edge shrinks as you scale while costs do not. Real capacity needs hundreds of names and hard per-name limits, not 50 large caps.

  • The short side may not be implementable. Half the book is short. In India that means intraday cash short (no overnight hold, so you forfeit the overnight residual), borrowed stock (limited inventory and a fee that eats the thin edge), or single-stock futures (only F&O names, subject to position-limit bans and roll costs). If you cannot short a name cleanly, the book stops being market-neutral. Treat the short leg as a research abstraction unless you have a real implementation path.

  • Factor crowding and decay. Cross-sectional reversal is one of the most well-known, most-arbitraged signals on the planet; the edge is thin because it is crowded. In a deleveraging event it can flip violently to momentum as every desk unwinds the same residual losers at once - the August 2007 quant quake is the textbook case. A backtested gross Sharpe is an upper bound that crowding erodes in real time.

  • Beta-estimation error. "Market-neutral" is only as good as the rolling beta. A 120-day beta is stale after a regime change, noisy for short-history names, and wrong around corporate actions - so the residual carries leftover market and sector exposure, and the supposedly neutral book quietly takes directional risk it never priced.

  • Survivorship and a point-in-time universe. This is today's index looking back. A genuine cross-sectional book trades the changing membership, including names later dropped or delisted, which lowers the gross signal below what this flattering panel shows.

This is the most scalable idea in the course and, in the same breath, its clearest lesson. The signal is real; the residual genuinely reverts; the book is genuinely neutral. And after the toll of harvesting it at realistic NSE cost, it is a clean research signal with no tradeable edge. It becomes interesting only if you can simultaneously drive per-side cost below ~2.66 bps and implement the short side for real - and until both are true, the honest description is exactly that. The next chapter turns to the risk and sizing machinery that decides whether any of these signals, gross edge and all, survives as a real book. Educational content only, not investment advice.

14.html:
A single pair is a coin with a hidden bias and a habit of breaking. The previous chapters built one - HDFCBANK / KOTAKBANK - and then dismantled it: held out blind, net of realistic costs, its out-of-sample Sharpe was 0.34, an honest near-flat curve. The beginner's instinct is to hunt for a better pair. The desk's instinct is the opposite: stop betting the book on any one relationship, and build a portfolio where no single pair can sink you. This chapter takes six same-sector, cointegrated-ish pairs and does the real engineering - neutralise what you do not want, size each to a risk budget, weight by risk rather than gut, and put hard rules underneath. The honest headline up front: six pairs lift the out-of-sample net Sharpe from 0.34 to 0.43 and shrink the worst drawdown from -13.8% to -10.0% in this data window. That is a steadier ride on the same marginal edge - not a new edge. Everything that follows is about earning even that.

From one pair to a book that survives

We do not cherry-pick a winner. We take a generous list of same-sector candidates, re-estimate everything on the train window (2019-2023) exactly as before, and keep the six with the lowest Engle-Granger cointegration p-value that also have a tradeable half-life (5 to 60 days). The hedge ratio, the trailing 60-day z-score, the next-bar fill - identical machinery to the single-pair notebooks, nothing peeking at the future.

Pair Sector Train coint p Hedge ratio Half-life
M&M / MARUTI Auto 0.0015 2.106 22.0
INFY / TCS IT 0.0017 1.431 24.5
TCS / HCLTECH IT 0.0217 0.682 21.3
HDFCBANK / KOTAKBANK Banks 0.0306 1.054 29.3
TATASTEEL / JSWSTEEL Metals 0.0356 1.052 45.9
HINDALCO / TATASTEEL Metals 0.0686 0.820 38.9

Look at the deliberate flaw baked in: the two IT pairs both touch TCS, the two metals pairs both touch TATASTEEL. Those pairs are not independent bets - TCS being the short leg in one pair and the long leg in another wires a strong negative correlation between them. That is realistic, and it is the entire reason a book needs a covariance matrix and not just a list of single-pair vols.

The premise of bundling them is correlation, so measure it: the average pairwise correlation of the six gated pair P&Ls is just 0.04. They are nearly orthogonal - each earns in bursts and sits flat in between, and they do not all earn at once. That near-zero correlation is the engine that makes a book smoother than any line inside it.

Key idea

A pairs book is not a collection of bets - it is one bet on a correlation structure. At an average pairwise correlation of 0.04 the diversification is real, but it is also the most fragile assumption in the building: correlations that are near zero in calm regimes snap toward one in a crisis, exactly when you need them apart. The whole book leans on a number that is least reliable when it counts.

Sizing: volatility targeting and the shrunk covariance

The six pairs are wildly unequal in risk. A one-unit M&M / MARUTI spread, with a hedge ratio above 2, is several times more volatile than a one-unit HDFCBANK / KOTAKBANK spread. Drop one raw unit of each into a book and M&M alone owns the risk - "diversification" becomes a slogan. Volatility targeting fixes it: scale each pair by a constant leverage = target_vol / its_own_vol, estimated on the train window and frozen, so every pair contributes the same standalone risk (8% annualised). The leverages tell the story - M&M / MARUTI gets just 0.14 while TCS / HCLTECH gets 0.41 - the loudest pair is turned down the most.

Equalising risk lifts the book Sharpe from 1.10 (naive, one raw unit each) to 1.34 (vol-targeted), both scaled to the same 10% target so the comparison is purely about balance, not leverage. But vol targeting only fixes each pair's standalone risk; it is blind to how pairs co-move. That co-movement - the shared-leg correlations - lives in the covariance matrix, and a covariance matrix is estimated, which is where the trouble starts.

With six assets and a short window, the sample covariance is noisy and ill-conditioned, and an optimiser fed such a matrix produces garbage weights - it amplifies noise in the near-singular directions. The diagnostic is the condition number (ratio of largest to smallest eigenvalue; higher means closer to singular). On the realistic 63-day windows a desk actually re-estimates on, the sample matrix has a median condition number of 6.9 (max 16.7); Ledoit-Wolf shrinkage cuts that to 2.3 (max 8.2) - roughly a 3x improvement in stability, trading a little bias for a lot of conditioning. The heatmaps make the shared legs visible: the two IT pairs sit at a strong negative correlation through TCS, the metals pairs through TATASTEEL.

Heads up

Ledoit-Wolf makes the matrix better conditioned, not correct. It buys you stability, not foresight. The correlations that drive every weight below - especially the shared-leg ones - drift over time, so a covariance optimised on 2019-2023 is a snapshot, not a law. Shrinkage stops the optimiser from exploding on noise; it does not stop the world from changing.

Weighting by risk, not by gut

With a trustworthy covariance you can weight pairs by risk. Two classic long-only schemes, weights summing to one, under a hard concentration cap of 35% so no pair can dominate: minimum variance (put weight where it lowers total book variance most - it loves the negatively-correlated IT pair that hedges its sibling) and risk parity / equal risk contribution (ERC) (every pair supplies the same share of book volatility). Minimum variance gives the lowest model vol by construction (2.55% vs 2.77% for equal weight); ERC sits a touch higher at 2.60%, buying balance with a sliver of variance. The reason ERC matters is the gap between equal capital and equal risk.

Under naive equal weighting the risk contributions are lopsided: HDFCBANK / KOTAKBANK supplies 26.0% of book volatility and M&M / MARUTI 23.7%, while the two IT pairs supply only 9.7% and 8.6%. Two pairs out of six carry half the risk. After vol targeting the diagonals are roughly equal, so this imbalance comes almost entirely from the correlations - the IT pairs hedge each other and contribute little net risk, while the standalone bank and auto pairs contribute a lot. Risk parity flattens every contribution to exactly 16.7% (1/6), and the concentration cap is the backstop underneath: no pair exceeds 35% of capital, so one broken pair can never take the book down alone.

Rupee-neutral is not beta-neutral

Now the exposures. A long/short book has two size numbers: gross exposure (sum of the absolute legs - what you actually trade, pay costs on, and margin against) and net exposure (the signed sum - your directional tilt). Gross breathes with how many pairs are firing (mean 1.08x, peaking at 2.80x) while net hugs zero (mean +0.017x), because every pair is internally long-short. That looks market-neutral. It is not. "Equal money long and short" is rupee-neutral; "equal market sensitivity long and short" is beta-neutral, and the two diverge the moment two legs have different betas to NIFTY. The pair beta is beta_A - hedge_ratio x beta_B, so a large hedge ratio amplifies any mismatch.

M&M / MARUTI is the cautionary tale: its pair beta to NIFTY is -1.11, while every other pair sits between -0.17 and +0.27. Summed across active pairs, the book's residual beta has a mean of just +0.008 but a standard deviation of 0.095 and peaks at |0.28| - a "market-neutral" book quietly carrying a quarter-unit of market risk whenever the high-hedge-ratio pair is on. The fix is a NIFTY overlay: short the lagged residual beta in the index each day, and the realised rolling 120-day beta collapses toward zero. Sector neutrality is cleaner by construction - every pair lives inside one sector, so net tilts stay tiny against gross (IT: 0.464 gross, 0.019 net; Metals: 0.372 gross, -0.003 net) - though the metals pairs sharing TATASTEEL are where a small net tilt can leak in.

Assemble it all - vol-targeted returns, risk-parity weighted, scaled to 10% portfolio vol, charged realistic delivery (CNC) costs of about 47 bps per leg-notional on every position change - and the full-sample net Sharpe is 0.94. Out of sample it is 0.43, total +9.0%, max drawdown -10.0%: steadier than the single pair, but the out-of-sample haircut is the same brutal fact it always was.

Note

Rupee-neutral is the exposure you can see; beta-neutral is the one that hurts you. The overlay zeroes the in-sample residual beta - out of sample the betas drift and the book carries market risk again, worst from the high-hedge-ratio pair, exactly when the market moves most. You are not removing the risk; you are removing the part of it you could measure on history.

The stop-loss-on-a-spread dilemma

On a single instrument a stop-loss is plain risk control. On a mean-reverting spread it is a paradox: a spread stretched to |z| > 3 is, by the strategy's own logic, more likely to revert - so the stop fires precisely when expected forward return is highest. Cut it and you bank a loss right before the snap-back; hold it and a truly de-cointegrated pair runs against you without limit. This is not a tuning problem; it is structural.

The most painful real instance in this book (excluding the COVID crash, a genuine break) is concrete: HDFCBANK / KOTAKBANK, short the spread, entered 2020-10-07, stopped out two days later on 2020-10-09 for -6.4%. Had you held, the spread reverted to the mean by 2021-01-05 for +21.4%. The stop did not save you - it cost 27.9% of the missed reversion, cutting at the worst possible moment.

The same tension runs through drawdown control. A causal throttle - cut book exposure to half once it is 5% below its high-water mark, restore above -2% - clips the tail from -10.0% to -8.6%, but it is not free: Sharpe falls from 0.94 to 0.76 and total return from +89% to +60%. It cannot prevent the first leg of a drawdown and it can whipsaw. You are buying a smaller tail with a lower Sharpe.

Key idea

No stop threshold and no drawdown throttle resolves the dilemma - they only choose which error to make. The stop that costs you a 21.4% reversion is the same stop that caps an unbounded loss on a pair that has genuinely de-cointegrated. Tuning the threshold on history just overfits which of the two mistakes you happened to make last time. Risk control here is the deliberate purchase of one regret to avoid a worse one.

Retiring a broken pair before it bleeds you

The hardest risk rule in stat-arb is also the most important: drop a pair when its cointegration dies. A spread only reverts while the relationship holds; once it breaks, the z-score machine cheerfully keeps generating entries into a trend that never comes home. We monitor each pair with a rolling 252-day Engle-Granger p-value and apply hysteresis to avoid flip-flopping - retire a pair after its p-value sits above 0.10 for three consecutive readings, re-admit only after it drops below 0.05 and holds.

The result is humbling, and it is the truest number in the chapter. Each pair passes the rolling screen only a sliver of the time - TCS / HCLTECH the most at 13%, INFY / TCS the least at 2% - and the median number of pairs passing the screen is 0 of 6. A disciplined book, obeying its own retire rule, sits empty more often than not. Read plainly: these relationships are cointegrated occasionally, not durably, and a static six-pair book that ignores the screen is mostly running on faith - trading regimes that have already ended because the entry logic never asked whether the tether still held.

Heads up

Retiring is late by construction. A 252-day rolling p-value confirms a break only after enough damage accumulates to move a year-long window - by the time the rule retires a pair, most of the loss is already taken, and re-admission can whipsaw you back into a still-broken relationship. Detection lags the event it detects. This is the best honest tool available and it is still a rear-view mirror.

Where this breaks

A portfolio layer makes a stat-arb book survivable; it does not make a weak edge strong, and every tool above leans on estimates that are themselves fragile.

  • The covariance is estimated, and it moves. Ledoit-Wolf cut the 63-day condition number from 6.9 to 2.3 - better conditioned, not correct. The shared-leg correlations that drive the weights drift, so a weight optimised on 2019-2023 can be wrong out of sample. Shrinkage buys stability, not foresight.
  • Neutralisation is backward-looking. The leg betas, hedge ratios and the NIFTY overlay are all fitted on history. The residual beta we "hedged to zero" is zero only in-sample; out of sample the book carries beta again, and the high-hedge-ratio M&M / MARUTI pair (beta -1.11) leaks the most exactly when it hurts.
  • Stops and throttles are double-edged. The stop that cost a 21.4% reversion is the same one that caps an unbounded loss on a truly broken pair. No threshold resolves the trade-off; tuning it on history overfits last time's mistake.
  • Retiring lags. A rolling cointegration screen confirms a break only after a 252-day window moves - the loss is largely taken before the rule fires, and re-admission can whipsaw. By that screen the median tradeable count was 0 of 6.
  • Six survivors, weighted in hindsight. This book is built from today's index names that happened to test cointegrated on the train window - the same survivorship bias the single-pair teardown quantified, now compounded across six pairs, with the delisted and merged pairs invisible.

The honest summary: this is research-grade portfolio construction on a research abstraction. It is the right way to turn signals into a book, and it materially improves the ride - out-of-sample net Sharpe from 0.34 to 0.43, worst drawdown from -13.8% to -10.0%. But it sits on top of the same marginal, cost-laden, drifting edge, and it inherits every caveat of the equity-short problem, which on Indian markets may require stock futures, borrowed stock, or intraday square-off - each changing costs, margin, borrow availability and risk. A statistical relationship existing is not the same as a tradable edge existing. The portfolio layer is what keeps you in the game long enough to find out which one you have. Educational content only, not investment advice.

15.html:
A backtest is a measurement contaminated by the act of searching. Every threshold you tried, every window you swept, every pair you scanned spends a little statistical significance, and by the time a strategy "works" you have usually tested it into existence. The previous chapters took the HDFCBANK / KOTAKBANK pairs trade out of sample once and watched the seductive in-sample net Sharpe of 1.01 collapse to 0.34. That was the first line of defence. This chapter is the full validation scorecard - the battery of tests a quant runs before believing a single number, run on the same pair and the same z-score rule, with the real numbers in this data window. The point is not to bless the backtest. It is to subtract the part that was never real and report what little remains without flinching.

The validation funnel: each ring subtracts edge that was never real

A single backtest answers exactly one question - could a rule have fit this past? - and the answer is almost always yes. Validation is the funnel that asks the harder questions in sequence, and each one removes apparent edge. What drips out of the spout is the honest part. Hold this picture for the whole chapter: the wide mouth is the seductive in-sample Sharpe, and every ring below it is one section that follows.

The machinery on trial is the same as before: a log-price spread s = A - b.B, the hedge ratio b estimated on the 2019-2023 train window and frozen, a trailing 60-day z-score, enter at plus-or-minus two, exit at the mean, hard-stop at four, next-bar fills, and realistic delivery (CNC) costs on every position change. Everything downstream validates the one daily net-return series that produces. Keep two reference numbers in mind throughout: in-sample net Sharpe near 1.0, out-of-sample near 0.3.

Walk-forward: the distribution, not the average

One out-of-sample number is a single draw from a noisy process - quote it alone and you are bluffing. Walk-forward turns the held-out test into many out-of-sample folds: re-estimate the hedge ratio on data up to a cut-off, trade the next block blind, step forward, repeat. The anchored scheme grows the training window from a fixed start; the rolling scheme uses a fixed-length trailing window that forgets old regimes. The honest output is never the average - it is the distribution of fold Sharpes.

Across 14 folds, the anchored scheme lands a median fold Sharpe of +0.47 with an interquartile range of [+0.29, +1.10]; 93% of folds are positive, but the worst is -0.96 and the best a fluky +3.30. Rolling does slightly better in the middle (median +0.57) and much wider at the tails (IQR [+0.10, +1.67], worst -0.95). The shape is the tell: this is not a tight cloud sitting confidently above zero, it is a low pile of mediocre folds with a couple of lucky spikes dragging the mean up. And the headline in-sample 1.01 sits above almost every single fold. A lone backtest reports the best case, not the typical one.

Heads up

A single out-of-sample Sharpe is one sample from a wide distribution. When the median fold is roughly half the headline, a quarter of folds are near-flat, and the worst loses money outright, the right summary is "around 0.5, but I have seen -0.96," not "0.57." Anyone who shows you one number has chosen which draw to show.

Purged and embargoed cross-validation: ordinary k-fold leaks

The natural next thought is k-fold cross-validation: instead of one cut, use many folds and average. But ordinary k-fold leaks on a trading strategy, and the leak is structural, not a bug you can patch. Two threads tie nearby days together. First, overlapping labels: a reversion trade opened on day t is judged by the spread's move over the next H days, so day t's outcome is built from the same returns as t+1, t+2, and so on - here the holding horizon is about H = 39 days. Second, trailing features: today's z-score is computed from the prior 60 days. If you then shuffle observations into folds at random - the default in almost every CV library - nearly every test day has a near-duplicate neighbour sitting in the training set, and the model is tested on data it has effectively already seen.

Measured deterministically on this strategy, the fraction of test days with a training neighbour inside the label window is 100% for shuffled k-fold, 16.6% for blocked (contiguous) folds, and 0% once you purge plus-or-minus H days and add a small embargo. That leak is not cosmetic - it inflates the score. Selecting the entry threshold on each training fold and scoring it out of fold, the naive shuffled-CV Sharpe is 0.78; switch to purged and embargoed folds and it drops to 0.50 - a 55% inflation removed, and the longer the holding horizon, the bigger that gap.

Key idea

The k-fold leak is not a coding mistake you can fix with a cleaner library - it is baked into the fact that trading labels overlap in time. Shuffle the rows and you hand the model near-duplicates of the test set. Half the apparent cross-validated edge here was that leak, and it vanished the moment the folds were made contiguous and purged.

The Deflated Sharpe Ratio: pay a toll for every trial

Suppose you searched 64 configurations and reported the best one. Its Sharpe is biased upward by the very act of taking a maximum over many noisy trials - even with zero true edge, the best of 64 random strategies looks good. The Deflated Sharpe Ratio (Bailey and Lopez de Prado) prices this in. It compares your observed Sharpe not against zero, but against the Sharpe you would expect to beat by luck alone after N trials, and it corrects the significance test for non-normal returns.

Here the best of 64 configs has a daily Sharpe of 0.070 (annualised 1.11), with heavy non-normality - skew +1.54, kurtosis 18.0 - over T = 2347 days. Tested against zero, the Probabilistic Sharpe Ratio is 1.000: it looks rock-solid, indistinguishable-from-certain. But the expected maximum Sharpe from 64 lucky trials is 0.56 annualised, and deflating against that benchmark instead of zero drops the Deflated Sharpe to 0.962. It still clears the conventional 0.95 credibility line - just. Try a few hundred configurations rather than 64 and the same observed Sharpe falls straight through 0.95 into "not credible." The toll scales with how hard you searched.

Heads up

The Deflated Sharpe here charged for 64 explicit configs. It cannot see the pairs you quietly discarded, the windows you tried last week, or the thousand backtests in published papers you read before choosing this one. The true number of trials - and therefore the true haircut - is always larger than any you can write down. A PSR of 1.000 against zero is meaningless if you do not also state how many doors you opened to find it.

Probability of Backtest Overfitting

The Deflated Sharpe asks "is this number real?" PBO asks the deeper question: is my whole selection procedure overfit? Using Combinatorially-Symmetric Cross-Validation (Bailey, Borwein, Lopez de Prado, Zhu), we take the T x N matrix of trial returns, chop time into S blocks, and for every symmetric way of splitting the blocks into an in-sample half and an out-of-sample half: pick the best in-sample config, find its rank out of sample, and record the logit of that rank. PBO is the fraction of splits where the in-sample champion lands below the out-of-sample median.

Over 70 symmetric splits of S = 8 blocks across the N = 64 configs, PBO = 54%. The config that looks best in sample is below median out of sample more often than not - barely better than picking a config at random. The scatter makes it concrete: the IS-best config carries a median in-sample Sharpe of +1.25, but its median out-of-sample Sharpe is +0.66, and the cloud sprays across the diagonal rather than hugging it. Selection added almost no out-of-sample information.

Note

A PBO near 50% is the statistical signature of a search that has learned the noise, not the signal. It does not mean the strategy is worthless; it means the act of choosing the best config gives you essentially no edge over a coin flip. If your ranking of configurations does not survive being re-ranked out of sample, you have not found a setting - you have found a story.

What survives, and how sure we are

Two questions remain. How wide is the error bar on the surviving Sharpe, and is the edge a fragile ridge or a robust plateau? Take the error bar first. The Sharpe is a point estimate with a fat tail, and on roughly 600 out-of-sample days that tail is wide. A stationary block bootstrap (Politis and Romano) resamples blocks of consecutive days - preserving the autocorrelation and clustering of real returns - a few thousand times, and reads the Sharpe off each resample.

The out-of-sample point Sharpe of 0.34 comes with a 95% bootstrap interval of [-0.47, 1.15], and zero sits squarely inside it - we cannot reject "no edge" at 5%. The probability the true Sharpe is negative is 20%. Even the full-sample interval, [0.30, 1.31], is wide enough that the point estimate is far less certain than a single clean number ever admits. The honest read is not "Sharpe 0.34" but "somewhere between losing money one year in five and a respectable edge, and I cannot tell which from this data."

Now the shape. Sweeping the net Sharpe across entry-versus-exit and window-versus-entry slices, the base config sits on a broad, gently varying plateau, not a thin spike: the base full-sample net Sharpe is 0.82, its 3x3 neighbourhood averages 0.79 with a standard deviation of only 0.08, and 96% of all swept cells are positive. That is the good news - the specific thresholds were not curve-fitted to a coincidence. The bad news is that it is a low plateau, and the one-knob-at-a-time table shows the out-of-sample Sharpe is still contingent on choices: shortening the z-score window to 90 days lifts out-of-sample Sharpe to 0.99, while 60 or 120 days leave it at 0.34, a tightened stop drops it to 0.19, and doubling the assumed cost takes it to 0.22. Robustness to small changes is real here, but it is the robustness of a marginal edge.

Put every layer on one page and the story is plain. Each ring of the funnel removed apparent edge, and what reaches the spout is thin and uncertain.

Validation layer What it asks Result in this window
Single in-sample backtest Could a rule fit the past? Net Sharpe 1.01
Walk-forward (anchored) Survive re-estimated blind folds? Median fold 0.47, 93% > 0, worst -0.96
Walk-forward (rolling) Same, adapting to drift Median fold 0.57, 86% > 0, best +3.07
Purged + embargoed CV Strip the overlap leak 0.78 -> 0.50 (55% inflation removed)
Deflated Sharpe (64 trials) Beat the best of N lucky tries? PSR-vs-0 = 1.000 collapses to DSR 0.96
PBO via CSCV Is selection itself overfit? 54% - a coin flip
Block-bootstrap 95% CI How sure is the Sharpe? 0.34, CI [-0.47, 1.15], zero inside
Stability sweep Ridge or plateau? Plateau, 96% cells > 0, base only 0.34 OOS
Key idea

Validation working looks like a disappointment. The job of these tests is not to bless the curve - it is to subtract the part that was never real and report the residue. On this pair the residue is a thin, uncertain, out-of-sample edge whose confidence interval brackets zero. That is the method succeeding, not failing.

Where this breaks

Validation is a defence, not a guarantee, and each tool here fails quietly in its own way. An expert keeps the failure modes in view.

  • Validation has its own overfitting. Run walk-forward, PBO, and the rest, then tweak the strategy until they pass, and you have simply overfit to the validators. These tools work only when run once, on a hypothesis fixed in advance. Used iteratively they become another search you must deflate.
  • The number of trials is always underestimated. The Deflated Sharpe charged for 64 explicit configs. It cannot price the pairs you discarded, the windows you tried last month, or the published papers that steered you here. The true N, and the true haircut, is larger than anything you can write down.
  • Purging assumes you know the holding horizon. We purged plus-or-minus 39 days from a half-life estimate. If a spread refuses to revert and positions persist longer, the embargo is too short and the leak survives. Purged CV controls overlap, not the deeper non-stationarity that walk-forward targets.
  • The bootstrap assumes the past distribution is the future. A stationary block bootstrap resamples the regime you observed. It cannot manufacture the crash you did not see, the borrow squeeze, or the day the cointegration simply ends. Its interval is a lower bound on your uncertainty, not the whole of it.
  • PBO and stability maps are relative, not absolute. A low PBO and a broad plateau say that within your grid selection is stable - they say nothing about whether the entire grid is a basin of in-sample luck. A robust, well-validated, marginal edge is still marginal.
  • None of this restores the real-world reality. A strategy can pass every test on this page and still be untradeable once borrow fees, financing, position-limit bans, two-legged execution risk, and a point-in-time universe are imposed. Treat the short leg as a research abstraction unless you have a valid implementation path.

The bottom line: on HDFCBANK / KOTAKBANK, what survives walk-forward, purged cross-validation, the Deflated Sharpe, a 54% PBO, a bootstrap interval that brackets zero, and a low plateau is a thin, uncertain edge that the data cannot reliably distinguish from nothing. The rarest skill in quantitative trading is the discipline to compute all of this before you fall in love with a curve - and to walk away when the funnel comes up empty. Educational content only, not investment advice.

16.html:
Every earlier chapter handed you a research object: a cointegrated spread, a trailing z-score, a net-of-cost Sharpe that survived an honest out-of-sample window. None of those is a position. The gap between a surviving hypothesis and a managed book on NSE is not one decision - it is a pipeline of them, and most of the ways to lose money hide in that pipeline, not in the signal. This chapter is the unglamorous machinery: which vehicle carries each leg, what legging actually costs when two orders cannot fill at the same instant, how wide the spread and impact really are when measured from bars instead of assumed, and the monitoring, kill switch and retirement discipline that decide whether an edge survives contact with reality. Nothing here sends an order - this is education about implementation, not a recipe for placing trades. The whole point: a statistical relationship is not a tradable edge, and even a tradable edge dies without this scaffolding.

The pipeline is a loop, not a launch button

Beginners build the left half of the system - data, signal, backtest - and skip the right half, which is the half that keeps you solvent. Implementation is a loop: a net-positive hypothesis chooses a vehicle for each leg; the vehicle dictates execution (legging, slippage, participation, square-off); execution feeds monitoring (the running spread, z-score, exposure, PnL); and monitoring either keeps the trade on or retires it and sends the idea back to research - never straight back to the order book.

Picking the vehicle: the holding-period crossover

The long leg is trivial - buy the cash name for delivery and hold. The implementation problem lives in the short leg, where there are three honest vehicles, each a trade-off you choose rather than a default. Cash intraday carries no overnight cost but forces a square-off near 3:20pm - so you re-cross the full spread every day just to stay in. SLB-borrowed stock pays a borrow fee that is lumpy and scarce: many names have no borrow at all, and the fee can spike to double digits exactly when everyone wants the same short. Stock futures are the workhorse - one entry and exit cost, a small running carry, a roll every expiry, and a basis that wobbles around spot.

The decisive variable is how long you must hold. Intraday re-crossing is a straight ramp; the once-paid vehicles start higher but barely grow. Price the crossover directly from the cost model and the line is stark: with a per-round-trip transaction cost of about 28 bps (four fills - two legs, entered and exited), intraday re-crossing overtakes futures after roughly one day of holding.

Key idea

The vehicle is dictated by holding period, not preference. A sub-day mean-reversion signal can use cash intraday; anything held for days or weeks - which is most cointegration trades, with their multi-day half-lives - must use futures or SLB, because paying the full spread twice a day to stay in a multi-week trade is ruinous. The futures line embeds a roll every roughly 30 days, and its real enemy is not this transaction cost but basis wobble and lot-size granularity that the price series never showed you.

That last point is the quiet one: you estimate the hedge ratio on cash closes but trade it in futures that sit off spot, round to fixed lots, and price an expected dividend the cash series already removed. Each is a small, structural tracking error between the spread you backtested and the one you trade - always there, not noise you can diversify away.

Legging risk: two orders that must both happen

In a backtest both legs fill on the same bar at the same price. In reality you send leg A and leg B, one fills first, and in the gap before the other fills, the market moves. That gap is legging risk, and it produces four distinct failures: slippage on the second leg as it drifts away while you chase it; partial fills that leave you off-ratio; hedge-leg failure, where one leg fills and the other is rejected or untradeable and you are now naked directional - the exact risk the pair was built to remove; and outright order rejection from margin, a price band, a freeze quantity or an F&O ban.

Put a number on it with a Monte Carlo calibrated on real intraday data. Measured 5-minute return volatility is 13.1 bps, which works out to about 0.757 bps per second of unhedged exposure; simulate thousands of legged entries against a spread-only floor of 5.2 bps and the distribution is not the floor. The median legged entry costs 7.8 bps, the 95th-percentile fill costs 16.2 bps - a bad-gap fill at multiples of the spread - and 24% of entries cost more than twice the spread floor. The histogram is right-skewed because the gap drift is random but you systematically chase into the move. And this is entry only: the exit legs the same way, doubling the legging drag over a round trip.

The two defences pull in opposite directions. Market orders guarantee both legs fill but pay the full chase; limit orders cap the slippage but raise the chance leg B never fills - the worse failure, because it leaves you naked. And "naked" is not a rounding error: at a generous 95% per-leg fill probability you still end up unhedged about 9.5% of the time - one trade in ten - rising to 18% at 90% per leg. That is not a small directional bet; it is a full single-stock position the strategy never sized for.

Heads up

The most expensive outcome in pairs trading is not slippage - it is being unhedged. A 95%-reliable fill on each leg leaves you naked roughly one entry in ten, and that tail is not in any slippage histogram. The legging simulation prices the gap; it cannot price the regime where leg B is simply unavailable - no borrow, an F&O ban, a locked circuit - and you sit directional in a position you never wanted.

Costs you can least defend: spread and impact

The cost model has been carrying assumed values for the half-spread and impact - the two numbers you can least defend in advance, because they move with your size, the time of day and how the book looks when you trade. So measure them. There is no quoted bid-ask in OHLCV, so estimate the effective spread with the Corwin-Schultz high-low estimator, which recovers the bid-ask from consecutive bar ranges. Across the liquid large-caps the effective spread clusters tightly - 4.6 to 5.8 bps, a representative median around 5.3 bps. The naive fallback, when intraday is unavailable, is the daily high-low range - and it is wrong by an order of magnitude, about 33 times the effective spread, because it bundles the whole day's volatility into "spread".

Impact is the other half. Model it as square-root in participation - the price moves roughly as the square root of how much of the bar's volume you take. Calibrated on each name's daily volatility, that is about 8.3 bps at 1% participation, 18.5 bps at 5%, and 26.1 bps at 10%. The curve is convex-down: doubling your size does not double the impact, but it never stops growing either.

That convexity is the entire argument for a participation cap - a hard rule never to take more than X% of a bar's volume. A reusable slippage function that charges spread plus impact per fill prices the trade-off: about 10.4 bps per fill at a 1% cap, 27.3 bps at 10%. A tight cap (2-5%) protects your price but stretches the exit across more bars - and a long exit is exactly when a circuit, a gap or a borrow recall can trap you mid-unwind; a loose cap (10-20%) gets you flat fast but pays the convex impact and leaks information. No setting is both cheap and fast.

Now assemble one honest number. A pair round trip is four fills, and each pays statutory charges, half the measured spread, and the modelled impact at the participation cap. Stack them and the total round-trip cost is about 123 bps - 1.23% of one leg's notional. That is the hurdle every signal must clear before there is a single rupee of edge. If your backtested edge per trade is, say, 30 to 50 bps, this one number decides whether the strategy lives or dies.

Note

Every cost here is an estimate, and estimates flatter. The Corwin-Schultz spread is inferred from OHLC, not quoted; the impact model is a square-root assumption with a chosen coefficient; the legging vol is one name's recent tape. When the assembled hurdle is 123 bps and a plausible per-trade edge is 30 to 50 bps, the right question is not "is it profitable?" but "how wrong can my cost estimate be before the sign flips?" - and the answer is: not very.

Monitoring and the kill switch

A managed pair is not fire-and-forget. The minimum viable system is a small pipeline: a data feed drives a signal engine; every order passes a risk-and-limits gate before it reaches the order path; and a monitoring loop watches the running state - spread, z-score, exposure, PnL, a both-legs-on check, margin headroom - with a kill switch wired straight to a flatten action.

The kill switch is not a vague intention to "watch it" - it is a small set of pre-decided gates, any one of which flattens the book. Implemented as pure logic that decides but never sends an order, the gates evaluate on every tick: a healthy pair returns OK; a spread blow-out to a hard limit (in the sample, |z| = 4.8) returns FLATTEN because the relationship may have broken rather than reverted; a broken leg returns FLATTEN because a naked single-stock position must go now.

Before any of this touches a broker, the entire loop runs in sandbox trading (analyzer mode in OpenAlgo), where the same code path simulates fills and returns order objects without sending anything. Conceptually the order path has two shapes: a discrete order per leg, carrying a product (MIS intraday or NRML carry) and a price_type (MARKET or LIMIT); and a smart, idempotent order that reconciles a symbol to a target position size - the natural fit for a pair that must hold a neutral ratio and be flattened to zero. We describe the signatures only; nothing here is an instruction to place a trade.

Key idea

A kill switch is only as good as the feed and the discipline behind it. A stale tick, a missed alert, or a single human override "just this once" defeats the whole apparatus. The intraday square-off clock is the same kind of hard rule: flatten on your schedule before the auto-square-off window, never at the market's worst liquidity on the broker's clock. The switch must be mechanical and pre-committed, or it is decoration.

Retiring a strategy when the edge dies

The hardest gate has no dramatic trigger: a slow death. An edge erodes as others find it, as the relationship weakens, as costs rise. Take a real pair, fix the hedge ratio on the early window (here beta = 1.08 on the first two years), and watch the rolling co-movement and spread stability degrade. The diagnosis is blunt: the 120-day return correlation spends 48% of the history below 0.5 - nearly half the time, the two names are not even moving together at the level the trade assumed. When co-movement spends long stretches under your threshold and the spread keeps widening instead of reverting, the relationship that justified the pair is decaying.

The correct action is not to size up and "wait for reversion" - that is the gambler's move, doubling into a dead edge while it bleeds 123 bps every round trip. It is to retire the strategy and send the idea back to research; the data, not your hope, makes the call. That is the loop closing - monitoring detected decay, the retire gate fired, and the idea returns to research, not to the order book.

Where this breaks

  • Every cost here is an estimate, and estimates are optimistic. The Corwin-Schultz spread is inferred from OHLC, not quoted; the impact model is a square-root assumption with a chosen coefficient; the legging Monte Carlo is calibrated on one name's recent 5-minute vol. Real fills in thin names, fast tape or a stressed market are worse - sometimes far worse - and the 123 bps hurdle is the floor, not the ceiling.
  • The legging simulation assumes you always get both legs. It prices the slippage of a gap, not the regime where leg B is simply unavailable - no borrow, an F&O ban, a locked circuit - and you sit naked. A 95% per-leg fill still leaves you naked roughly one trade in ten, and that tail is not in any histogram above.
  • The carry-cost crossover hides a scarce-borrow reality. The SLB line uses one tidy fee; in practice borrow is lumpy, recallable mid-trade, and absent for exactly the names you most want to short. The futures line ignores margin financing and gap-day margin calls that can force a de-gross at the worst moment, plus the structural tracking error of basis, lot rounding and dividends.
  • The kill switch and monitoring are only as good as the feed and the discipline behind them. A stale tick, a missed alert or a human override defeats the whole apparatus. The switch must be mechanical and pre-committed, or it is decoration.
  • No backtest, however honest, equals a real track record. The research-to-reality gap - queue position, partial fills, rejections, latency, basis, recalls, regime change - only reveals itself with small size, in sandbox first. Sandbox results are a hypothesis about execution, not proof of it.

The final, honest word: a statistical relationship is not a tradable edge, and a tradable edge, once found, is not permanent. What stands between a clean research curve and a surviving book is the unglamorous machinery of this chapter - the right vehicle, controlled legging, measured costs, hard participation caps, square-off discipline, monitoring, a pre-committed kill switch, and the maturity to retire a strategy when its edge is gone. Build that machinery, or do not trade the edge at all. Educational content only, not investment advice.

17.html:
Sixteen chapters ago we made a single promise: to draw one bright line, between a statistical relationship and a tradable edge, and to never let a pretty number talk us across it. Everything since has been the machinery for holding that line - cointegration tests, spread construction, signal design, cost models, out-of-sample discipline, and a parade of techniques that looked brilliant in-sample and humiliating out of it. This final chapter does not teach a new trick. It assembles every earlier piece into one disciplined workflow you can run end to end, then reads back the honest scorecard of what actually survived. The arc has been deliberately brutal because the subject is. What is left at the end is small, hard-won, and real - and knowing exactly how small is the whole point.

One chain, six links

A stat-arb programme is not a strategy; it is a pipeline, and every link can quietly break the one after it. Get the universe wrong and your cointegration test is mining noise. Trust correlation instead of cointegration and you select pairs with no anchor. Freeze a hedge ratio and the spread mis-hedges the moment the regime turns. The workflow below is the same chain the companion notebooks walk, in order, with no step skippable.

What each link actually taught

Data and universe come first because every statistic downstream inherits their flaws. We restricted the search to liquid, F&O-eligible names in the same sector - an economic reason to co-move - and used point-in-time, split- and dividend-adjusted closes. The closing price is the right learning signal (one clean, comparable, auction-settled print per name per day, exactly what a cointegration test wants) and the wrong execution price (you cannot transact at an adjusted close, and your real fill lands on the next bar). Holding both ideas at once is the discipline the whole course is built on.

Cointegration, not correlation, is the entry exam. Correlation is a returns-level statistic about today; cointegration is a price-level statement about the long-run tether. They are not two flavours of one idea - across 75 same-sector pairs the rank correlation between return-correlation and the cointegration p-value was -0.04, statistically indistinguishable from zero. The most correlated pair in the table drifted apart and never came back; weaker-correlated pairs reverted cleanly. Screen on the wrong one and you systematically select spreads with no centre of gravity.

Finding pairs without fooling yourself is mostly defence against your own search. Run a cointegration test over hundreds of pairs and a handful pass at 5% by pure chance - the same spurious-regression machinery that makes 90% of unrelated random walks look "significant" on price levels. The honest screen starts from an economic prior, controls the false-discovery rate, and treats any low p-value pulled from a big scan as a hypothesis, never a discovery.

Spread and signal is the only genuinely mechanical step: an estimated cointegrating hedge ratio (not the price ratio, which silently assumes a beta of one), a trailing rolling z-score windowed to the spread's own half-life, and a four-zone rule - enter in a band, exit at the mean, hard-stop beyond it - filled on the next bar, never the same one. Assembled cleanly, this prints a gorgeous in-sample curve: on HDFCBANK against KOTAKBANK, +98% gross at a 0.99 Sharpe, a 72% hit rate, and zero stops triggered. Every number true, in that window, in-sample, gross. None of it a forecast.

The honest scorecard

Then comes link five, where most ideas die, and where the course earns its keep. We took every seductive in-sample result out of sample, subtracted realistic NSE costs on two legs traded twice, and asked whether the relationship was even stable. Here is the whole arc on one page.

Technique Headline (in-sample / gross) Honest verdict (net, out-of-sample)
Naive pairs (HDFCBANK / KOTAKBANK) net Sharpe 1.01 net Sharpe 0.34 - two-thirds gone
Dynamic hedge (Kalman beta) promised adaptivity OOS net -1.50 vs static +0.76; 0 of 20 grid settings beat the static beta
Basket (Johansen, 5 banks) 94% of sub-baskets stationary 0% still stationary out-of-sample
Cross-sectional factor-neutral gross Sharpe 1.34 net Sharpe -1.99 under turnover
Portfolio of 6 vol-targeted pairs net Sharpe 0.94 net Sharpe 0.43 out-of-sample

Read that table slowly, because it is the course. The naive pair was not a bug - it was a clean, correct build whose edge was mostly a property of the window it was tuned on; held blind it kept barely a third of its Sharpe, and the pair failed the cointegration test in about 90% of rolling windows. The Kalman filter, the "obvious" fix for a drifting hedge ratio, did not just underperform the frozen beta - it lost money out of sample, and no setting on a 20-point grid cleared the static bar, because a hedge ratio that chases noise mis-hedges more than a stale one. The Johansen basket was the sharpest trap: 94% of candidate baskets tested stationary in-sample and exactly none survived, because a k-leg vector has k-1 free weights and estimation error is where baskets go to die.

The flagship pair makes that deflation concrete - every honest step, taken in order, knocks the seductive number down toward zero. Nothing here is a leak; it is the same strategy seen through progressively less flattering glass.

The cross-sectional book is the most instructive failure of all, because its signal was real.

Heads up

The cross-sectional, factor-neutral book ranks the whole universe and is the form the large quant desks actually run. Its gross edge here was genuine - a broadly monotonic +3.89 bps/day top-minus-bottom decile spread, a Sharpe of 1.34 gross. And it still lost: net Sharpe -1.99, because the signal demanded roughly 235x annual one-way turnover, and a gross 2.48 bps/day cannot out-run a 6.45 bps/day cost. The breakeven per-side cost was 2.66 bps against a realistic 6.9 bps. A real edge that inverts under its own turnover is not an edge - it is a toll you pay the market.

Key idea

Four families of technique, one verdict: a statistical relationship existing is not the same as a tradable edge existing. Naive pairs were marginal net and out of sample; the dynamic hedge lost to the static beta; the basket overfit to zero; the cross-sectional gross edge inverted under turnover. The risk-managed six-pair book is the closest thing to a survivor - a net out-of-sample Sharpe near 0.43 - and even it is a marginal, fragile, costs-and-borrow-sensitive number, not a money machine.

This is the funnel every notebook from chapter three onward is really one layer of: thousands of pairs co-move, fewer are cointegrated in-sample, fewer still stay cointegrated out-of-sample, fewer again survive realistic costs, and only what is left at the very bottom is implementable and net-positive.

What would make a model genuinely tradable

The scorecard is not a counsel of despair - it is a specification. A model that clears every bar above is rare, but it is not mythical, and the four things that separate it from a backtest artefact are concrete.

A real short-leg vehicle. Every backtest in this course shorted freely; that is the single largest abstraction. In Indian cash equities an intraday short is force-squared off near the close, so overnight reversion cannot be carried that way. The realistic default is the stock future - liquid in large names, naturally short-able - but it drags in basis, a monthly roll cost, lot-size granularity, margin on each leg, and the Market-Wide Position Limit: cross 95% of it and the name enters a ban period where you cannot add, often exactly when the spread is most crowded. SLB borrow is the cleanest economic match but is thin, lumpy and recallable. No short, no market-neutral trade.

Costs modelled net, on two legs, both ways. A pair round trip is eight fills, each paying statutory charges plus half the spread plus impact. The honest number ranges from tens of basis points for a delivery hold to over 120 bps once you model real effective spreads and impact on two legs intraday. The edge must clear all of it before a rupee is yours - and the cross-sectional book proves a real gross signal can sit entirely below that line.

Capacity you can actually fill. A signal that needs more size than the thinner leg can absorb is a paper edge. Liquidity is the first filter, not an afterthought - the span across even a blue-chip universe is wide, and a pair that yokes a liquid name to a thin one inherits the thin one's execution problems on both legs.

Validation that does not flatter. Naive cross-validation leaks through overlapping labels (Sharpe 0.78); purged-and-embargoed CV stripped that to 0.50. Deflate the best-of-many-trials Sharpe for the search itself and the Deflated Sharpe sits at 0.96 with a 54% probability of backtest overfitting - barely better than a coin flip that the search found anything real. The out-of-sample Sharpe of 0.34 carries a 95% bootstrap interval of [-0.47, 1.15] that comfortably contains zero, with a 20% probability the true Sharpe is negative. A single backtested number without that uncertainty beside it is the optimistic end of a range, not the answer.

Tip

The honest workflow inverts the usual leaderboard. Start from an economic prior, not a scan. Pre-register the rank, the vector and the parameters before touching out-of-sample data. Prefer the fewest legs that capture the relationship - every added weight is estimation variance and cost. Walk forward, re-estimate, and accept the turnover and overfitting that re-estimation brings. Then validate against the search, not just against the past. The technique that survives all of that earns a small allocation; the one that merely won the in-sample beauty contest earns a place back in research.

The pre-trade checklist

Before a single rupee of real capital touches a stat-arb idea, it passes nine gates. A NO on any one of them does not mean trade smaller - it means the idea goes back to research, not to the order book.

Note

Notice how few of these nine gates are about statistics. Gates one through three and six through nine are market microstructure, borrow, margin, operations and tax - the off-page costs that never appear in a price series and that quietly decided every verdict in the scorecard. The cointegration test you spent six chapters mastering is, at most, gate four's poorer cousin. The edge is won or lost in the boring gates.

Run the engine yourself

None of this is meant to be taken on faith - the entire research engine is open for you to run, break and modify. The companion Jupyter notebooks in the Statistical Arbitrage folder of the OpenAlgo Tutorials repository are self-contained and reproduce every number in this course on real NSE closes: 00b is the research-to-real bridge and this checklist; 02 through 06 build stationarity, cointegration, pair-finding and the signal; 07 is the brutal reality check; 08 is the Kalman that lost; 09 the Johansen baskets; 10 the cross-sectional book that inverted; 11 risk and portfolio construction; 12 the honest validation; and 13 the implementation pathways. Change the pair, change the window, change the cost assumption, and watch the verdict move. That movement is the lesson: a result that flips when you nudge a window was never a result.

Strip away every flattering layer and one curve is left - the flagship pair, net of realistic costs, out of sample, every drawdown intact. It is the only line that corresponds to money you could actually have kept, and it is the one an honest researcher leads with, not the one a brochure crops out.

Where this breaks

Even this synthesis is provisional, and the honest caveats are the same ones that have shadowed every chapter.

  • Every number is in-this-window. Different universes, windows and cost models give different verdicts. The scorecard is a faithful account of one decade of NSE data, not a law of markets. Re-run it on yours.
  • The short leg is still assumed. The whole course shorts freely on equity closes. Real implementation needs a futures, SLB or intraday vehicle, each with basis, roll, borrow, margin and ban risk the backtests never paid.
  • Survivorship flatters all of it. The universe is today's index members looking back; the pairs that merged or delisted never entered the search, so even the humbling numbers are the flattering version.
  • The survivor is marginal, not safe. The risk-managed book's out-of-sample Sharpe near 0.43 is the best honest result in the course, and it is still thin, costs-sensitive, borrow-dependent and one regime shift from breaking. A small allocation, watched closely, with a kill switch - not a livelihood.
  • Crowding makes the edge shared. Thousands of desks run the same screens; market-neutral to the index is not neutral to other arbitrageurs deleveraging into you.

If you take one sentence from seventeen chapters, take the one the whole course was built to earn: a statistical relationship is not a tradable edge. Educational content only, not investment advice.
